text:
Meaning of Research: A Historical Perspective for Sports Science
Research is essentially a systematic, purposeful inquiry to generate new knowledge. In sports science, this means applying scientific methods to ask and answer questions about physical activity and performance. As Sharma (2023) notes, research is a “formal, systemic and intensive process” of using the scientific method.

It involves a careful attitude of enquiry: asking questions no one has asked before, then assembling facts to answer them (Rusk, cited in Sharma, 2023). In practical terms, research follows concrete steps formulating a hypothesis or question; collecting data (e.g.  through experiments, observations, surveys); analyzing that data to test the hypothesis; drawing conclusions; and comparing results to existing theory. This structured approach - an “attitude of enquiry” and “scholarly application of the scientific method” (Moule, cited in Sharma, 2023) is what gives research its power to add knowledge.

Steps of the research process: A typical outline is 
(1) Pose a question or hypothesis; 
(2) Collect data (measurements, observations, experiments); 
(3) Analyze data to test the hypothesis; 
(4) Interpret results and draw conclusions; and 
(5) Relate findings to theory

Example: A coach who hypothesizes that a different running technique will improve speed might conduct timed trials, record performance data, analyze results statistically, and conclude which technique is better. This small-scale process is research in action.
Early Inquiry: Trial and Error in the Stone Age
Long before formal science, our Stone Age ancestors were effectively doing research through trial and error. For example, early hominins shaped sharp stone tools (“knapping”) over millions of years by experimenting with flaking techniques. These stone blades were not invented in a single “Eureka” moment, but refined gradually as humans tested which rocks made the best cutting edges. 
Such experimentation – trying different flake angles or materials to improve a spear point – exemplifies primitive research. Likewise, early humans learned by doing: a hunter who adjusted throwing techniques or changed spear designs was gathering empirical data on what works. Over generations, this trial-and-error learning (e.g.  observing that throwing a spear at a higher angle hits farther) accumulated into systematic knowledge. In fact, researchers today note that creating sharp stone tools was a long process tied to practical needs (“no reason to produce sharp stone tools unless the need to cut is already in place”.
These early activities laid the groundwork for later science. By experimenting with fire, tracking prey, or improving tool shapes, prehistoric people were essentially building an inductive understanding of cause and effect. They passed this knowledge on informally – for instance, a young hunter learns how far to throw a spear to reach a target – which is the ancestor of organised research. In sports terms, imagine a runner in ancient times changing stride length to see what felt fastest; this simple test-and-learn process is the seed of scientific inquiry in human movement.

Classical Civilizations: Greek and Roman Insights
By classical times (ancient Greece and Rome), people began to record and discuss knowledge systematically. In Greece, philosophers and physicians connected the body and mind and even treated athletes, blending practice with theory. Hippocrates (c.460–370 BC) – the “Father of Medicine” – advocated regular exercise to maintain health. One of his teachers, Herodicus, was a gymnastic instructor (paidotribes) who pioneered “therapeutic gymnastics” for Olympians. 

In other words, Greek experts were already studying how exercise influenced well-being. Although Aristotle (384–322 BC) lacked modern anatomy tools, he systematically studied animal movement to infer human anatomy, starting comparative biomechanics. Even Plato, in ‘The Republic’, argued that citizens should train both mind and body. Thus, the Greeks saw physical training as tied to ethics and health:  mens sana in corpore sano (“a healthy mind in a healthy body”) was an implicit guiding idea.
In Rome and beyond, this tradition continued. The physician Galen (2nd century AD) wrote extensively on exercise, diet and muscle, further spreading early exercise science Greek exercise culture (gymnastics, Olympic games) itself stimulated inquiry: athletes had trainers and physicians who observed injuries and performance. (For example, ancient sports medicine treated Olympians and gladiators – even then, experts worked to return athletes to competition). Throughout these civilizations, knowledge was still often tied to philosophy or religion, but a shift was happening: medicine and exercise were being studied based on observation and experience rather than just superstition.
Example in context: In ancient Greece a physician like Hippocrates would recommend jogging or wrestling exercises for patients. His successors recorded these practices as part of “sports medicine,” building a primitive but systematic understanding that exercise affected health. This reflects early research: trial programs of exercise, observation of outcomes (fewer aches, better stamina), and advice based on those observations.
The true birth of modern research methods came with the Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment (17th–18th centuries). Thinkers like Francis Bacon, René Descartes and later John Locke and Isaac Newton insisted that knowledge be based on evidence and experiment. Enlightenment writers declared that any claim must be “verifiable, capable of practical demonstration”; otherwise it should be treated as mere hypothesis or fable. The famous motto became “Prove it!” – every theory had to be proven by repeated observation or experiment. Sir Isaac Newton’s laws and method of repeating experiments with the same results gave enormous prestige to this empirical approach.
This shift had direct consequences for all sciences, including movement science. The scientific method – formulating hypotheses and testing them – was formalized. Researchers kept detailed records and used logical reasoning to draw conclusions. By the late 18th and 19th centuries, this meant that even studies of human movement became quantitative. For example, early physiologists began measuring pulse rates and breathing during exertion. In 1860, Edward Hitchcock Jr.  (an Amherst College professor of physical education) coauthored one of the first textbooks on exercise physiology, and Austin Flint Jr. published on how exercise affected the heart. These pioneers exemplified the new spirit: they measured, recorded, and reported exercise outcomes in a systematic way.
Enlightenment principles: Knowledge must be tested against reality. Propositions are accepted only if experiments consistently support them. The priority is on evidence: “Bacon, Newton and Locke were hailed as the guiding spirits of empiricism and the scientific method”. In practice, this led to controlled trials and systematic observation. For sports science, it meant moving beyond anecdote to measurement –for instance, timing runners with chronometers or using spring scales to measure force.
Modern Sports Science: Data, Technology, and Evidence-Based Practice
Today’s sports science fully embodies this empirical tradition, employing advanced technology and rigorous methods. Researchers use statistics, experiments, and data analytics to understand human performance. A striking example is wearable technology: devices like GPS trackers, inertial sensors and heart-rate monitors collect real-time data from athletes during training and competition. 
For instance, modern trackers (PlayerLoad™) measure an index of mechanical stress by combining accelerometer data, allowing coaches to quantify exactly how hard an athlete has worked. Such data-driven methods enable immediate adjustments (e.g. slowing a sprinter if fatigue indicators rise) and long-term optimizations.
At the same time, laboratory research has exploded. Biomechanics labs use high-speed cameras and force plates to analyze movement; exercise physiologists measure VO₂max (maximum oxygen uptake) and lactate thresholds to tailor training; and psychologists run controlled trials on motivation and skill acquisition. Importantly, sports science is held to high statistical standards: as one review notes, “robust statistical validation” is required for publication in top journals. 
This means studies must have proper controls, reliable measurements, and replicable methods. The field even faces unique challenges: elite athletes are few, so researchers often work with small sample sizes, yet still apply rigorous analyses.
Crucially, modern sports science integrates health and performance. For example, wearables and AI-driven analytics are now used not only for training but also to monitor sleep, nutrition, stress and recovery. Data streams on an athlete’s sleep patterns or dietary intake can be combined with training data to predict and enhance performance. Research frameworks propose a “precision sports science” approach– continuously updating models of each athlete with new data to personalize coaching.
The result is an era of evidence-based practice: coaches and trainers make decisions grounded in data and peer-reviewed findings, just as clinicians rely on medical research. In essence, today’s sports scientist is an applied researcher, blending physiology, biomechanics, psychology and data science to improve athletic outcomes.
Example: National teams now employ sports scientists to quantify training load and injury risk. A common strategy is the acutely chronic workload ratio (ACWR): research has shown that sudden spikes in training volume increase injury risk (Gabbett, 2016).  Using daily session data from wearables, a coach can calculate an athlete’s ACWR and adjust training intensity if the ratio is too high. This is directly applying contemporary research findings to practice, illustrating how modern methods inform sports training.
Conclusion
From prehistoric hunters experimenting with tools to today’s analysts crunching athlete biometrics, research has always been about asking questions and learning systematically. The meaning of research in our field is this continuous quest: we ask how and why the body moves and responds. Over millennia, our methods have evolved from informal trial-and-error to rigorous scientific protocols, but the goal remains the same – to expand knowledge that can improve performance and health. By understanding this history, we appreciate that contemporary sports science stands on centuries of inquiry: our sophisticated data models and lab experiments are the heirs of those very first experiments by the fireside. In class and in practice, students should remember that every experiment or data analysis they conduct carries forward this ancient tradition of inquiry, always aiming to turn observations into new insights (Sharma, 2023).

text:
RESEARCH PAPER WRITING TIPS 

At present research paper writing is a significant part of all academic curriculum and the students must write a research paper during their educational period. Here, the main aim of writing a research paper is for improving the skill to make use of collected information to support your point of view on a particular topic. As a researcher you want to have a solid grip in the fundamental ideas and terms used in the respective field that you are writing. It is simple to write a good research paper if you understand the writing. It is also simple to write a good research paper if you understand the writing pattern of a research. One of the important things to do before setting out to write your paper is to make your time schedule. Research paper writing includes many tasks such as thesis writing, collection of needed information, preparation of draft, etc, as such; it should take more time. You need to split the time allowed for your research paper to various processes. If the target period for your research paper is three months, then schedule the first ten to fifteen days for managing your thesis statement, the next fifteen days for collecting the useful resources to hold up your thesis and the following days for your writing and editing process. Choosing of a right topic is the next important thing to follow a research paper writer. You should select a topic which is interesting to you to make the entire research process straightforward. And confirm that you know something related to the topic for developing your point of view on that topic. This will help you to save your time from a deep research. Collect needed information to support your thesis using various resources available. Make use of great books related to the topic, academic publications, magazines and internet resources such as educational websites and blogs of educational institutions and government authorities. Move to your writing processes with the collected resource. Go through your point of view on the topic and use the appropriate resources to support it. Try at most to give a variation to your research writing to give a better attraction to the final paper. If it is not clearly maintained, the allocation of collected information will make bad effect in your entire research writing. Your attitude towards the research writing process is the key factor which determines the final result of your research. If you pay good attention, surely you will get a great result too. 9 In writing research papers, it is important to prevent some very common errors by avoiding these research papers writing blunders enumerate below; 2.1 LETTING YOUR DEADLINE SLIP One never means to do it, but somehow deadlines often creep up out of nowhere. For big research papers, try to make a timeline in your planner. Schedule dates that you would like to have certain tasks completed (such as your outline and first draft) and maintain your progress. For instance, as earlier stated, if you are targeting three months for your research paper, then schedule the first ten to fifteen days for managing your thesis statement, the next fifteen days for collecting the useful resources to hold up your thesis and the following days for your writing and editing process. Making strides ahead of time will keep the stress and late- nights to a minimum as the turn- in date draws nearer. 2.2 BEING AFRAID TO ASK FOR HELP Everyone struggles with research paper writing from time to time. As a student, your professors, supervisors, and librarians want you to succeed and, more than likely, they will all be eager to help you or give advice where they can. Don’t hesitate to contact your professor/supervisor if you are not sure of how to get started or how to progress with your paper; they are there to help you. Schedule a meeting and bring along some of the work you have done so far. You may be able to divide some of your research tasks into small chunks which can be tackled whenever you have a little spare time. For example, if you take photocopies of materials you need to read, be sure not to wait until the last minute to ask for help with things you should have ordinarily begun long ago. As a researcher, it is always advisable to hook-up with a research assistant or a consultant. You cannot know it all, there must be an area you would need an expertise advice to achieve the desired quality required. 2.3 MAKING YOUR TOPIC TOO BROAD Once you start developing ideas for your research paper, try to narrow your focus down even further. Papers that lack focus only skin the surface of a number of concepts, but never go into the details. Challenge yourself to pick one very specific thesis and research as much as possible 10 to inspire you to flush out your ideas as best as you can. The thesis should serve two purposes, it should guide you while you write and guide the readers when they read. The thesis should have a strong argument based on a debatable claim”. Researchers need to review some existing literature in their interest area in order to be focused on the interest topic, this review will expose you to what others have been able to do in the same or similar area as such; could help you to modify your topic and make it a peculiar one. 2.4 SKIMPING ON THE SOURCES Providing credible sources and a lot of examples is a great way to let your audience/professor know that you have actually performed thorough research and really understood the topic. 2.5 GETTING UNORGANIZED Doing great research may not assure you a great research paper if you cannot keep your materials organized enough to call upon them later. You can even highlight the most important parts of the source, make personal notes on the margins, and automatically create your bibliography – all right on your computer. It is also possible to become unorganized within your very paper. It is important to ask yourself the following questions to keep your focus:  Did I use my outline?  Is there anything that I missed?  Is there sense to my arguments?  Did I put everything in my own words?  Do my paragraphs connect and flow from one to the other?  Does my conclusion tie the entire paper together? 2.6 BEING SNEAKY Changing the margins? Adjusting the line spacing? Altering the font size ever so slightly? Resist the temptation to do this. When a template is given or when your professor sets a page length it is not because they enjoy reading that specific page length in particular. It is because your professor knows that page requirement is the amount of space that it will take to develop your topic to the appropriate amount and earn the good grade you deserve. Following these advices will assure that you do not need to try any of those tricks. 11 2.7 ADDING “FLUFF” Sometimes meeting that page length requirement can be difficult when you feel as though you have run out of things to say. Professors know fluff when they see it. They know you have chosen to ramble on to fill the page requirement rather than do more research. Your introduction and your conclusion should not generally exceed one page unless your paper is extremely long. If you hit a wall, consider asking your professor for advice on where else you might go with this topic or suggestions for further reading. 2.8 NOT HAVING A FRIEND READ IT OVER Sometimes when you have spent so many hours and days on a project, your exhausted eyes can start to miss things. This concept may not make sense to anyone else, though it is necessary. Have someone else do a read through of your paper, even if it is a quick one, to make sure that your paragraphs are coherent and you have not made any obvious mistakes. Also, consider visiting your school’s writing center for further help if they offer one. The more reviews, the better. Sometimes there are centers that offer such services, register with them to get these necessary services. Remember these sayings: nothing good comes easy, penny wise pound foolish. Do not be too stingy with money; use it to obtain the necessary services it is meant for to avoid making avoidable mistakes. 2.9 TRUSTING THE COMPUTER TO SPELL – CHECK Just because you have managed to avoid those red squiggles while typing your research paper does not mean your paper is error – free. Your computer will not always let you know when you have used the wrong form of a word such as “you’re” or “their” but your professor certainly will. Carefully read over your entire paper when you have finished to be sure you have avoided simple spelling and grammatical errors. Professors hate reading silly mistakes as much as you hate making them. 2.10 FORGETTING FINISHING TOUCHES Often for big projects such as a research paper, professor will have very specific guidelines as to how they would like it submitted to them. Make sure your name, class, and date are on it and do not forget the staple! If your professor requested a cover page, binder, etc., do not forget to get 12 everything assembled ahead of time. You do not want to lose points on presentation after working so hard on the research. When it comes down to it, you know you are fully capable of writing a research paper worthy of an “A.” What you need to do is avoid these common mistakes and give yourself enough time to complete the assignment to the best of your ability. These back to school tips will help you learn how to write a research paper and feel confident doing so. (James,2013)

text:
INSTRUCTIONAL TECHNOLOGY IN PHYSICAL EDUCATION/HUMAN KINETICS EDUCATION AND SPORTS
Introduction
Instructional technology in Physical Education (PE), Human Kinetics Education, and sports involves the application of digital tools and innovative strategies to enhance teaching and learning outcomes. It encompasses a wide range of technologies, from basic audio-visual aids to advanced computer-based simulations and virtual reality environments. The goal is to improve instructional methods, facilitate better engagement, and enhance the overall learning experience for students and athletes. Instructional technology refers to the systematic use of technological resources to design, implement, and assess the instructional process. According to Reiser and Dempsey (2017), it includes the integration of multimedia content, interactive tools, and digital platforms to create an effective learning environment. In the context of PE and sports, instructional technology supports skill development, performance analysis, and theoretical learning through various technological interventions.
Instructional technology has the potential to revolutionize Physical Education, Human Kinetics Education, and sports by enhancing teaching methods, increasing student engagement, and improving performance outcomes. However, its successful implementation requires careful planning, adequate resources, and ongoing professional development for educators. By addressing these challenges, educators can leverage technology to create a more effective and enriching learning environment.
Application in Physical Education and Sports
Digital Video Analysis: One of the most significant applications of instructional technology in PE and sports is digital video analysis. Tools such as Dartfish, Coach's Eye, and Hudl allow for detailed analysis of athletic performance. By recording and reviewing their movements, students and athletes can receive precise feedback on their technique, enabling them to make informed adjustments and improvements (O'Donoghue, 2014).
Dartfish is a digital video analysis tool used primarily in sports for performance analysis. It allows users to capture, analyze, and share video content to enhance athletic performance. The tool includes features like slow-motion analysis, tagging of key events, drawing tools for technique analysis, and data overlays. (Visit the Dartfish website where you'll find images, videos, and detailed descriptions of the software. You can check also Google Images for "Dartfish video analysis tool" variety of screenshots and pictures. Check YouTube also for "Dartfish software tutorial")
Coach's Eye is a digital video analysis tool widely used in sports coaching to provide instant video feedback to athletes. It allows users to record, analyze, and share videos, often incorporating slow-motion playback, annotations, voice-over commentary, and side-by-side comparison features. (Visit the Coach's Eye website where you'll find images, videos, and detailed descriptions of the software. You can check also Google Images for "Coach's Eye video analysis tool" variety of screenshots and pictures. Check YouTube also for "Coach's Eye tutorial" or "Coach's Eye app review")
Hudl is a popular video analysis platform used by sports teams to review game footage, analyze performances, and share insights with players and coaches. It includes features like slow-motion playback, annotations, and the ability to create highlight reels. (check Google images, youtube and Hudl website for more details)
Wearable Technology: Devices like fitness trackers and smartwatches provide real-time data on physical activity, heart rate, and other vital metrics. This data can be used to monitor student progress, set personalized fitness goals, and adjust training programs accordingly. Wearable technology fosters a data-driven approach to physical education, promoting accountability and motivation among students (Santos et al., 2016).
Examples of Wearable Technology in Sports and Physical Education
Smartwatches and Fitness Trackers (e.g., Fitbit, Garmin, Apple Watch): These devices track metrics like heart rate, steps taken, calories burned, sleep patterns, and more. Provides athletes and PE students with real-time feedback on their physical activity, helping them monitor progress, set goals, and improve overall fitness.
GPS Tracking Devices (e.g., Catapult, StatSports): Used by professional teams and athletes to track movement patterns, speed, distance covered, and acceleration during training and games. Helps coaches analyze performance, optimize training loads, and prevent injuries by monitoring workload and fatigue levels.
Heart Rate Monitors (e.g., Polar H10, MyZone): These devices monitor heart rate in real-time, often paired with apps or displays to show how hard an athlete is working. Allows athletes to train within specific heart rate zones, optimizing cardiovascular training and ensuring workouts are conducted at the right intensity.
Wearable Biomechanics Sensors (e.g., Zepp, Motus): Sensors attached to the body or equipment (like a baseball bat or golf club) to capture data on motion, swing speed, angle, and force. Provides detailed biomechanical analysis to help athletes refine their technique and improve performance while reducing the risk of injury.
Smart Clothing (e.g., Hexoskin, Athos): Clothing embedded with sensors that monitor physiological metrics such as heart rate, respiratory rate, muscle activity, and body temperature. Offers a comprehensive analysis of an athlete's physical condition during training or competition, allowing for real-time adjustments and post-activity review.
Concussion Detection Sensors (e.g., Prevent Biometrics, Shockbox): Worn on the head or integrated into helmets, these sensors detect and measure impacts that could lead to concussions. Provides immediate alerts to coaches and medical staff when a dangerous impact occurs, enabling quick response to potential head injuries.
Wearable Hydration Monitors (e.g., Nix Hydration Biosensor): Sensors that monitor hydration levels by analyzing sweat composition in real-time. Helps athletes maintain optimal hydration levels, preventing dehydration and improving performance and recovery.
Recovery Wearables (e.g., WHOOP, Oura Ring): Devices that monitor recovery by tracking sleep patterns, heart rate variability, and other indicators of recovery status. Allows athletes to optimize their recovery strategies, ensuring they are rested and ready for peak performance.
Smart Insoles (e.g., Arion, RunScribe): Insoles embedded with sensors that track foot pressure, gait, and running dynamics. Provides valuable data for improving running technique, reducing the risk of injury, and enhancing overall performance.
Posture Correction Wearables (e.g., Upright Go): Small devices worn on the back to monitor posture and provide feedback when the user slouches. Helps improve posture during physical activities, reducing the risk of strain or injury, and enhancing overall performance.
Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR): VR and AR technologies offer immersive learning experiences that can simulate real-world sports scenarios. For instance, VR can be used to create virtual training environments where students practice specific skills without the constraints of physical space or equipment. AR, on the other hand, can overlay digital information onto the physical world, enhancing understanding and engagement during physical activities (Feng et al., 2020).
Virtual Reality (VR) in Sports and Physical Education
STRIVR: Is used by NFL teams and college football programs in US, STRIVR provides immersive VR experiences where players can practice their decision-making and reaction skills in a simulated game environment without physical wear and tear. STRIVR helps athletes improve cognitive skills by simulating game scenarios, allowing them to "experience" the game off the field.
EON Sports VR: It offers VR training programs for baseball, soccer, and football players. Users can practice pitches, hits, and other skills in a virtual environment. Enhances muscle memory and technique by providing realistic, repetitive training scenarios.
Virtual Reality Fitness Apps: Apps like BoxVR and VR Boxing Gym provide fitness routines in a virtual environment, making physical exercise more engaging and fun. Encourages consistent exercise by gamifying physical workouts, making them more enjoyable and immersive.
ii. Augmented Reality (AR) in Sports and Physical Education
AR Coaching Systems: Systems like the AR sports coaching platform by AR Sports Coach provide real-time feedback and overlays during practice sessions. For instance, in golf, it can show the ideal swing path directly on the golf course through AR glasses. Allows athletes to receive instant visual feedback, helping them correct their form and technique on the spot.
Nike Training Club AR App: Nike has experimented with AR in its training app, where users can view exercises and get instructions overlaid onto their real environment. Enhances home workouts by providing guidance as if a trainer were physically present, improving form and effectiveness.
AR Spectator Experiences: During events like the U.S. Open, AR technology has been used to enhance the viewing experience for spectators. AR apps allow users to point their devices at the court and see real-time stats, player bios, and other information overlaid on their screens. Increases fan engagement and provides a more interactive and informative viewing experience.
AR Games and Physical Education Tools: Apps like "Pokémon GO" incorporate AR to encourage physical activity by having players walk around to capture virtual creatures in the real world. Encourages outdoor physical activity and can be used in physical education settings to make exercise more interactive and fun.
Online Learning Platforms: Platforms like Moodle, Blackboard, and Google Classroom provide a space for blended learning, where theoretical aspects of PE and human kinetics can be taught alongside practical sessions. These platforms support various multimedia resources, including instructional videos, interactive quizzes, and discussion forums, enabling a comprehensive learning experience (García-Peñalvo et al., 2015).
PE Central: PE Central is an online resource hub for physical education teachers, providing lesson plans, assessments, and professional development resources. It covers a wide range of activities and sports, catering to different grade levels. Physical Educators can access a variety of lesson plans, instructional videos, and activity ideas to enhance their PE curriculum and stay updated with the latest teaching strategies.
OpenPhysEd: offers a comprehensive collection of free physical education resources, including curriculum materials, lesson plans, and assessment tools. The platform focuses on promoting physical literacy and active lifestyles among students. PE Teachers can use OpenPhysEd to access ready-made lesson plans, download activity cards, and implement standards-based PE instruction in their classes.
Coursera: is an online learning platform that offers courses on a wide range of topics, including sports science, coaching, fitness, and health. Many courses are offered by top universities and can be audited for free or completed for a certification. PE teachers and coaches can enroll in professional development courses to enhance their knowledge of sports science, exercise physiology, and coaching techniques.
edX: is another online learning platform that offers courses from universities and institutions around the world. It provides courses related to sports management, fitness, nutrition, and physical education. Physical Educators can use edX to gain additional certifications or knowledge in areas such as sports psychology, biomechanics, or nutrition, which can then be integrated into their teaching practices.
Athlete Learning Gateway by the IOC: The International Olympic Committee (IOC) offers the Athlete Learning Gateway, an online platform that provides educational courses and resources for athletes and coaches. It covers topics such as sports performance, injury prevention, and mental health. PE teachers and coaches can access expert-driven content to improve their coaching methods and better understand the holistic development of athletes.
TeachPE: is an educational website that offers resources and study guides on various aspects of physical education, sports science, and coaching. The platform provides detailed information on topics such as anatomy, biomechanics, and sports psychology. This platform can be used by PE teachers to supplement their curriculum with in-depth content, create engaging lesson plans, and offer students additional study materials.
SPARK PE: provides evidence-based physical education programs designed to promote lifelong wellness. The platform offers online training, webinars, and resources for educators to implement effective PE programs. Physical Educators can use SPARK PE's resources to enhance their instructional strategies, access professional development, and apply research-backed methods to improve student fitness and activity levels.
National Academy of Sports Medicine (NASM): NASM offers online courses and certifications in fitness training, sports performance, and injury prevention. The platform provides comprehensive training programs for those looking to advance their careers in sports and fitness. PE teachers and coaches can pursue certifications through NASM to deepen their expertise in areas such as strength and conditioning, which can be directly applied to their teaching and coaching practices.
These online learning platforms provide valuable resources, professional development opportunities, and curriculum support for educators and coaches in the fields of sports and physical education.
Moodle: is a widely used open-source learning management system (LMS) that allows educators to create and manage courses online. It supports various multimedia formats, quizzes, forums, and assignments. PE instructors can use Moodle to organize course materials, deliver online lessons, manage student assessments, and track progress in a structured and interactive online environment.
Blackboard (Anthology): Blackboard is a comprehensive learning management system that offers tools for course management, virtual classrooms, and student engagement. It supports content sharing, online discussions, assessments, and grading. PE educators can utilize Blackboard to create and distribute digital content, facilitate online discussions, and assess student learning in a hybrid or fully online PE curriculum.
Google Classroom: Google Classroom is a free platform that integrates with Google Workspace for Education, allowing teachers to create, distribute, and grade assignments online. It also supports collaboration and communication between teachers and students. PE teachers can use Google Classroom to post announcements, share instructional videos, manage assignments, and encourage student collaboration on physical activity projects and health education topics.
Mobile Applications: Numerous mobile applications are designed to support PE and sports education. Apps like MyFitnessPal, Nike Training Club, and Strava offer workout plans, nutritional guidance, and performance tracking. These apps make it easier for students to engage in physical activities outside of school hours, fostering a culture of continuous improvement and self-directed learning (Domínguez et al., 2013).
MyFitnessPal: A popular fitness tracking app that helps users log their food intake and physical activity, providing insights into calorie consumption and overall nutrition. PE Educators can use MyFitnessPal to teach students about nutrition, track their physical activity, and encourage healthy lifestyle habits.
Strava: is a social fitness app that tracks running and cycling activities using GPS. It also allows users to compete with others and share their progress. PE teachers can use Strava to motivate students by creating challenges, tracking their running or cycling routes, and fostering a sense of community through shared achievements.
Nike Training Club: This app offers a wide range of workouts designed by professional trainers, covering strength, endurance, yoga, and mobility exercises. Physical Educators can integrate Nike Training Club workouts into their curriculum, offering students varied exercise routines that can be done both in class and at home.
TeamSnap: TeamSnap is a management app that helps coaches, players, and parents organize sports teams. It includes features for scheduling, communication, and managing team logistics. Physical Educators can use TeamSnap to organize intramural sports teams, manage schedules, and communicate with students and parents effectively.
Sworkit Kids: An exercise app designed specifically for children, offering fun and engaging workouts that promote physical activity. Sworkit Kids can be used in elementary PE classes to introduce young students to fitness through age-appropriate exercises that focus on strength, agility, and flexibility.
Fitbod: is a personalized workout app that adapts to the user's fitness level, equipment, and goals to create custom workout plans. Physical Educators can use Fitbod to design personalized fitness plans for students, particularly in high school PE, where individual fitness goals are emphasized.
Benefits of Instructional Technology in PE and Sports
Enhanced Engagement: Interactive and multimedia-rich content captures students' attention more effectively than traditional methods. Engaged students are more likely to participate actively and retain information better (Garris et al., 2002).
Personalized Learning: Instructional technology allows for the customization of learning experiences to meet individual student needs. Personalized feedback and tailored training programs can significantly enhance learning outcomes (Johnson et al., 2016).
Improved Performance: By providing precise data and detailed feedback, instructional technology helps students and athletes improve their performance. This can lead to better skill acquisition, increased fitness levels, and higher overall achievement (Liebermann et al., 2002).
Access to Resources: Technology enables access to a vast array of resources, including instructional videos, research articles, and online courses. This can enhance both teaching and learning by providing up-to-date information and diverse perspectives (Casey et al., 2016).
Challenges and Considerations
Despite its benefits, the integration of instructional technology in PE and sports education faces several challenges. These include: 
the cost of equipment, 
the need for technical training for teachers, 
and ensuring equitable access for all students. 
Additionally, there is a need to balance the use of technology with traditional physical activities to maintain the essence of physical education (Kretschmann, 2015).









IMPACTS OF TECHNOLOGY IN PHYSICAL EDUCATION
The integration of technology in physical education offers numerous benefits, including enhanced learning experiences, improved assessment methods, increased accessibility, and better communication. However, it also presents challenges such as dependence on technology, equity issues, and potential health concerns. Balancing the use of technology with traditional physical education methods and ensuring equal access for all students are crucial for maximizing the positive impacts of technology in this field.
Positive Impacts
Enhanced Learning and Engagement
Interactive Learning Tools: Devices like tablets and smartboards allow for interactive lessons, making learning more engaging. Apps and software can provide real-time feedback, personalized training programs, and interactive activities that enhance student participation.
Gamification: Incorporating games and challenges through apps and devices makes physical activities more fun and motivating for students. Tools like fitness trackers and virtual reality (VR) can gamify workouts, encouraging students to stay active.
Improved Assessment and Feedback
Data Collection and Analysis: Wearable technology such as fitness trackers and heart rate monitors provide accurate data on students' physical activity levels, heart rates, and other metrics. This allows teachers to assess performance more effectively and provide individualized feedback.
Digital Portfolios: Students can create digital portfolios to track their progress over time, setting personal goals and visualizing improvements. This can increase motivation and accountability.
Increased Accessibility
Online Resources: The availability of online tutorials, instructional videos, and fitness programs makes physical education more accessible. Students can learn new skills or engage in physical activities outside of school hours.
Adaptive Technologies: Technology can help cater to students with disabilities, providing customized physical education experiences. For example, VR can simulate environments where students with mobility issues can engage in physical activities.
Enhanced Collaboration and Communication
Social Media and Online Platforms: These tools enable students and teachers to share information, collaborate on projects, and participate in discussions related to physical education. This can build a sense of community and encourage peer support.
Remote Learning: In situations where in-person classes are not possible, technology allows for the continuation of physical education through live-streamed or recorded classes.
Negative Impacts
Dependence on Technology
Reduced Physical Interaction: Over-reliance on technology might lead to less face-to-face interaction and hands-on learning experiences. This can reduce the social and physical benefits of traditional physical education and sports.
Distraction: Technology can be a source of distraction if students focus more on their devices than on the physical activities.
Equity and Accessibility Issues
Digital Divide: Not all students have equal access to the necessary technology or internet connectivity. This can create disparities in learning opportunities and physical education experiences.
Cost: The implementation of advanced technology in physical education can be expensive, posing budget challenges for some schools.
Health Concerns
Screen Time: Increased use of screens can contribute to sedentary behaviour, eye strain, and other health issues. Balancing screen time with physical activity is crucial.
Injury Risk: Improper use of fitness technology without proper guidance can lead to injuries. For example, using VR for physical activities without adequate space or supervision could cause accidents.

University of Ibadan 
The Postgraduate College, Faculty of Education 
Department of Human Kinetics
Exercise Physiology 








Name- Elewi Ebenezer Mayowa 
Matric No- 198934








Course code - KHE 730
Course title - Physical Fitness, Weight Management and Relaxation 









  Assignment title - List ten (10) tests on a skill-related component of physical fitness, and explain any two (2)









			      Lecturer-in-charge - Prof. I.O. Oladipo
						SPEED

Speed is the rate at which an object or person moves from one point to another in a given amount of time. Scientifically, Speed = Distance ÷ Time.

Units;
Meters per second (m/s)
Kilometers per hour (km/h)
Miles per hour (mph)

The following constitute the types of speed that there are;
Acceleration Speed – How fast one can increase velocity.
Maximum Speed – Top running speed over a short distance.
Speed Endurance – Ability to sustain speed over time without slowing down.


Tests of Speed

In order to determine a person’s maximum speed, tests of speed should be short (<200 meters) and not involve changes of direction. Additionally, the acceleration phase of movement should be accounted for by setting a test length that allows the subject to attain maximum speed and maintain it for several seconds. Thus, the shortest speed tests are in the 30- to 40-yard range. This method of testing reduces the influence of factors such as fatigue or deceleration and will result in the most accurate determination of speed. The aim of all speed tests is to cover the set distance in the quickest possible time. Below is a list of ten (10) tests to determine the speed of an individual (Athlete);




10-Meter Sprint Test
Measures: Acceleration and initial sprint speed.

20-Meter Sprint Test
Measures: Short-distance sprinting ability.

30-Meter Sprint Test
Measures: Speed and acceleration

100-Meter Sprint Test
Measures: Maximum sprinting speed and speed endurance.

Repeat Sprint Ability (RSA) Test
Measures: Speed and ability to recover between sprints.

Radar Gun Speed Test
Measures sprinting speed using a radar device

60-Meter Sprint Test
Measures: Acceleration, top speed, and speed endurance.

Flying 20-Meter Sprint Test
Measures: Pure top-end speed (athlete gets a running start before the 20m is timed).

Sprint Fatigue Test (6 × 30m with short recovery)
Measures: Ability to maintain sprint speed under fatigue.

Resisted Sprint Test (sled or parachute)
Measures: Speed strength; how well an athlete sprints under resistance.


































				Repeat Sprint Ability Test

A repeat sprint test of anaerobic capacity, involving ten 20m sprints performed every 20 seconds. There are many similar repeat sprint tests, with varying sprint distance and recovery times. See also the similar sprint fatigue test (10 x 30m sprints every 30 seconds), sprint recovery (6 x 30m sprints every 20 seconds) and phosphate recovery (7 x 7-second sprints with 23 seconds recovery) tests. The following protocol was described in the document Tennis Australia National Physical Performance Protocols (2013).

Test purpose: this is a test of anaerobic capacity, the ability to recover between sprints and produce the same level of power repeatedly.

Target population: this test is suitable for athletes involved in many multi-sprint sports such as tennis, basketball, hockey, rugby, soccer, AFL.

Equipment required: 1 stopwatch and timing gates (or two stopwatches), measuring tape, marker cones, at least 20 meters of flat non-slip track. Timing gates are preferred for such short distance sprint tests as the margin of error can be significant.

Pre-test: Explain the test procedures to the subject. Perform screening of health risks and obtain informed consent. Prepare forms and record basic information such as age, height, body weight, gender, test conditions. Measure and mark out the test area. Perform an appropriate warm-up (see warm-up for sprints). See more details of pre-test procedures.

Procedure: 
Step 1 - marker cones, starting lines and timing gates are placed 20 meters apart to indicate the sprint distance. 
Step 2 - At the instructions of the timer, the subject places their foot at the starting line, waiting for the signal to 'go'. 
Step 3 - The stopwatch is started simultaneously with the first movement. The subject sprints maximally for 20m, ensuring that they do not slow down before reaching the end line. If a player's first sprint is not within 95 per cent of their previously recorded best 20 m sprint time, they will need to attempt the test again (after a 3-minute rest). If there are no timing gates, one stopwatch is used to time the sprint, the other continues to run. 
Step 4 - Record the time of the first sprint. The subject turns and makes their way to the end of the 20m track to run the next sprint in the opposite direction. Each 20-meter sprint starts 20 seconds after the previous run started. Players will be given a countdown with 10s, 3s, 2s and 1s remaining, before leaving on "Go". 
Step 5 - This cycle continues until ten sprints are completed, starting at 20 sec, 40 sec, 1 min, 1 min 20 sec, etc. after the start of the first sprint.

Scoring: All sprint times are recorded, and a Total Time (for the 10 sprints) is calculated. The percentage decrement is calculated by the following formula: (Total Time – (Best time x 10)) / Total Time x 100

Interpretation:
1. Best Sprint Time (RSA_best): This represents the fastest single sprint time during the test and reflects the athlete's maximum sprint speed. A faster time indicates a higher ceiling for sprint performance. 
2. Mean Sprint Time (RSA_mean): This is the average of all sprint times and indicates the athlete's general sprint speed over the course of the test. A lower mean time signifies better overall performance. 
3. Total Sprint Time (RSA_total): This is the sum of all sprint times and reflects the athlete's ability to maintain speed over multiple sprints. A lower total time indicates better fatigue resistance and recovery during the test. 
4. Sprint Decrement (RSA_decrement): This measures the percentage decline in sprint speed from the best sprint to subsequent sprints, often calculated as the difference between the fastest and slowest sprint or between the best and mean. A lower percentage decrement indicates better maintenance of speed and less fatigue. 
5. Qualitative Analysis: Beyond the numerical values, consider how the athlete's performance changes over the repeated sprints. Do they maintain speed well or experience a significant drop-off? Observing the pattern of sprint times can provide valuable insights into their fatigue resistance and recovery capacity. 
6. Contextualization: The interpretation of RSA scores should be done within the context of the athlete's sport and training goals. A high-performing athlete in a sport requiring repeated sprints might have different RSA values than an athlete in a sport with less emphasis on repeated sprints. 



					20m Sprint Test



Test Purpose: The aim of this test is to determine acceleration, and also a reliable indicator of speed, agility and quickness.

Required Equipment:

Test Procedure:
Step 1 - Participants should thoroughly warm-up prior to the commencement of the test. Warm-ups should correspond to the biomechanical and physiological nature of the test. In addition, sufficient recovery (e.g. 3-5 minutes) should be administered following the warm-up and prior to the commencement of the test.
Step 2 - Participants readies themselves on the start-line (positioned 0.3m behind the first gates – cones A) in a standing split-stance start position. (it is important for reliability that the participant always uses the same starting stance).
Step 3 - Participants should be counted down ‘3 – 2 – 1 – GO ‘.
If the test administrators are using a stopwatch, then the timekeeper must stand at the finish line and perform the countdown and time the sprint.
On the ‘GO’ signal the participant must accelerate maximally to the finish line as quickly as possible.
Step 4 - Each participant MUST complete a MINIMUM OF THREE SPRINTS, each separated by a 2-3 minute rest if reliable results are desired.
After the test.
Step 5 - Once the test is over, some subjects may react to the previous exertion. To reduce any problems, the subjects should rest, either sitting or standing, for at least 2-3 minutes. If the subject feels ill or goes quiet or pale, they should lie down with their feet resting on a chair. (never leave the participant alone after the test).

Scoring:
If three sprints were completed, all the scores are generated into a mean score which provides an overall 20m sprint time. This is done by using the following equation:
Mean score (seconds) = (#1 sprint time + #2 sprint time + #3 sprint time) ÷ total number of sprints (e.g. 3)

Interpretation: 
The normal result of the 20m sprint test can vary depending on factors such as age, gender, and fitness level. However, here are some general guidelines for interpreting the results of the test:

Elite male sprinters can complete the 20m sprint in around 2.7-3.1 seconds.
Elite female sprinters can complete the 20m sprint in around 3.0-3.4 seconds.
Well-trained male athletes can complete the 20m sprint in around 3.2-3.6 seconds.
Well-trained female athletes can complete the 20m sprint in around 3.5-3.9 seconds.
Average males can complete the 20m sprint in around 3.7-4.3 seconds.
Average females can complete the 20m sprint in around 4.1-4.7 seconds.

text:
Basic Economic Problems of Society and Resource Allocation
Introduction
Every society, whether rich or poor, faces the challenge of having limited resources to meet unlimited human wants. This situation of scarcity forces individuals, firms, and governments to make important choices about what goods and services to produce, how to produce them, and who should receive them. These choices form the basis of the basic economic problems of society. In this lesson, we will explore these fundamental problems and the factors that influence them, as well as how societies can use their resources efficiently to meet the needs of their people.

What to Produce
Every society must decide what types of goods and services to produce with its limited resources. This decision is necessary because human wants are unlimited, but the resources available to satisfy them-such as land, labour, capital, and entrepreneurship-are scarce. The question of what to produce involves choosing between various alternatives, such as whether to produce more food, clothing, roads, hospitals, or schools. For example, a country with fertile land may focus on agricultural products, while another rich in oil may produce petroleum-related goods. Ultimately, the goal is to allocate resources to produce the goods and services that are most needed or wanted by the people.

Factors that Determine What to Produce
Consumer Demand:
Producers are guided by what people in the society need and want. When there is high demand for a product or service, producers are more likely to focus on producing that item. For instance, if people are demanding more bread than rice, producers will shift their resources to bread production to satisfy the market and make profits.
Available Resources: The type of natural, human, and capital resources a country possesses greatly influences what is produced. A nation that has fertile land may focus on agriculture, while a country rich in crude oil may specialize in petroleum products. This ensures that production is based on the best use of available materials.
Government Policy: Government decisions and policies can shape production choices. If the government places a ban on the importation of rice and encourages local farming, producers will respond by focusing on rice cultivation. Policies like subsidies, tariffs, and taxes help direct producers toward specific sectors.

How to Produce
How to Produce refers to the decision a society or producer must make about the method or technique to use in the production of goods and services. It involves choosing between different approaches, such as using more human labour (labour-intensive method) or relying on machines and technology (capital-intensive method). For example, in countries where labour is cheap and abundant, producers may prefer manual methods, while in more industrialized economies, automated production may be more efficient. Ultimately, the goal is to select the most cost-effective and sustainable method that maximizes output while minimizing waste.

Factors that determine What to produce
Available Technology: The level of technology available in a country or business greatly affects how goods and services are produced. When modern machines and advanced tools are accessible, producers are more likely to use capital-intensive methods. However, in areas where such technology is limited, producers may rely on manual labour or traditional tools.
Cost of Production: Producers aim to minimize costs and maximize profit, so they often choose the method of production that is cheaper and more efficient. If using machines is more cost-effective in the long run than employing many workers, then capital-intensive production will be preferred. On the other hand, where labour is cheaper, producers may choose labour-intensive methods.
Nature of the Good: Some products require specific production methods depending on their characteristics. For example, handmade jewellery or custom furniture often requires skilled human labour, while factory-produced goods like plastic containers may be easily made using machines. The type of product being made influences the method used.
Labour Supply: The availability of workers can influence how goods are produced. In countries with a large number of unemployed people or a high population, labour-intensive production is often encouraged to create jobs. In contrast, countries with fewer workers may depend more on machines and automation.

THE IMPACT OF BOKO-HARAM’S INSURGENCY AND VIOLENCE ON WOMEN IN NORTH-EASTERN NIGERIA
It is a well-known fact that Nigeria as a country is facing different problems ranging from kidnapping, armed banditry, political assassinations, human trafficking etc. However, the most disturbing and even more dangerous problem is insurgency. Insurgency is a global phenomenon that impacts on lives and properties. Therefore, this paper attempts to examine the impacts of Boko Haram’s insurgency and violence on women in Northeastern Nigeria. The paper is anchored or explained better based on theoretical analysis. The problem of insurgency is virtually affecting all sectors of Nigeria, including security, education, economy, social political development, and more importantly its violence against the women-folk in Nigeria especially in North-Eastern part of the country. The paper posits that insurgency unleashes violence against women and remains a threat to safety of lives and properties of women. The study recommended that government should be proactive in dealing with security issues and violence through modern methods of intelligence gathering, training, and use of advanced technology. The paper also recommends that women in Nigeria should be given active roles to play in governance of this country.

text:
Meaning of Curriculum

The word “curriculum” comes from the Latin word “currere”, which means: i. ii. “to run or to run a course”. That is, a course of study, which contains a body of subject matter approved for teaching by society in schools. However, as said earlier, different experts view “curriculum” from perspectives other that the “course of study” definitions. Here are some of them; Curriculum is a “structured series of learning outcomes”. “output”. therefore tries to see curriculum as an “input” which results in a certain Another definition says: Curriculum is “an organized set of formal educational or training intentions”. It The emphasis of this view is clearly seen, from the “input” perspectives. In other words, what the school teaches or intends to teach is organized according to some set rules. For example, there is a principle which says that what is known should be taught first before proceeding to what is not known. Another example is the principle that what is simple should be taught first before teaching complex material. A pupil must learn first by counting concrete objects such as bottle tops- adding and subtracting them, before extrapolating to abstractions and more complex mathematical problems. 2 EDU 703 CURRICULUM DEVELOPMENT: THEORY & PRACTICE iii. iv. 3.2 Curriculum is a “deliberate, systematic and planned attempt undertaken by the school to modify or change the behaviors of the citizens of a particular society”. This view appears to be more elaborate in explaining the term “curriculum” than the others. Although the input-output paradigm is subsumed in this definition it is clear that what the school teaches must be deliberately planned and arranged in a particular way to bring about the desired outcome of changing the citizen’s behaviour. Analysis and Summary of the Definitions From the foregoing, it is clear that there is a programme which schools must run in order to properly educate the citizens. It is therefore a plan, a statement of purpose with a specification of content and methods, in- built with the capacity for evaluation to know whether the purpose has been achieved or not. This programme which the schools must follow or use has been carefully divided into three namely: i. ii. iii. Programme of studies – the subjects which must be taught in schools such as History, Geography, Economics and Physics. Programme of activities – planned and organized out-of – classroom teaching activities which help the learners to neither grow nor mature but are not formal as in classroom teaching setting. Examples includes getting students to join various clubs and societies in the schools (e.g. Sports, Historical Clubs, Debating Clubs, Dramatic Society etc)Through these, students learn through the process of socialization, because it is less formally strutted. Programme of Guidance – a programme of care for the well being of the learner. In this programme, professionally – trained helpers are employed to assist the learners achieving self- understanding and self direction necessary for adjustment to school, home and the larger environment. In specific terms, the counselors assist learners in three major areas: educational, vocational and persona-social. In educational areas, counselors help learners to take decisions on the right choice of vocational area, counselors assist learners to take decisions on the right choice of vocation or careers. In the persona-social area, counselors assist learners in handling emotional and psychological problems which may affect their behaviours adversely or prevent them from learning in schools. 3 EDU 703 CURRICULUM DEVELOPMENT: THEORY & PRACTICE In summary, the term “curriculum” may be said to be a total package of what schools do to make learners become what society expects them to become, namely good citizens, who are not only able to understand or learn certain school subjects, but fully integrated individuals that are able to fit into society and contribute their own quota as well, to the progress of that society. 3.3 Distinction between “Curriculum” and other Related Terminologies 3.3.1 Curriculum and Syllabus Most often people tend to equate the word “syllabus” with Curriculum”. This should not be so. As can be understood from explanations already given, curriculum is wider in scope than “syllabus”. A syllabus is part of a curriculum but it is not the Curriculum. Syllabus is the content of the school subjects offered in the school, and it is a sub-set of the curriculum. Such subject includes Mathematics, English Language, Biology and so on. A syllabus normally contains what students will learn in the various school subjects in a year or for a longer period of schooling leading to certification. It is a long term plan of work for students. It is normally prepared by the classroom teachers but a body such as West African Examination Council (WAEC) or the National Examination Council (NECO). At the university level, the National Universities Commission (NUC) presents syllabi (plural of syllabus) to all Nigerian universities. 3.3.2 Curriculum and Scheme of Work Again, the curriculum of a school is not the scheme of work. As the name implies, a scheme of work is a break down of the contents of what student are expected to learn in a given period. In other words, a scheme of work is the systematic arrangement of subject matter and activities within a given time period, such as a term or a semester. Whatever the learners are expected to learn are broken down into instructional units (which include activities) and are normally prepared by the classroom teacher. It is usually a guide in planning what is to be done per week over a term or semester and for the three terms or two semesters in an academic year as the case may be. 3.3.3 Curriculum and Course of Study A course of study is an educational programme leading to the award of a certificate at the end of the programme for a particular set of learners. 4 EDU 703 CURRICULUM DEVELOPMENT: THEORY & PRACTICE For example, a Nigeria Certificate in Education (NCE) is a course of study. Another example is a Bachelor of Education (B.Ed) programme in English, in a Teacher Education programme. Another example still is the Master of Science Programme (M.Sc.) in Plant Biology offered in a Faculty of Science in Nigerian Universities. A course of study therefore refers mainly to a programme of learning that are offered to students – with various course contents – at the end of which they are awarded a certificate indicating the type of course of study they had undergone. To understand this better, a friend might ask another what course he/she offered at the College or University. The answer could then be any of the following: a B.A. Ed course in Language Arts; an NCE course at the College of Education, an M.Sc course in plant Biology etc. 3.3.4 Curriculum and Lesson Note A lesson note (or note of lesson; or lesson plan) is a guide for teachers to assist them in the orderly presentation of a lesson to the learners in order to facilitate learning. Teachers draw the plan for teaching a particular lesson from the scheme of work. That is, just as the scheme of work is a breakdown of the syllabus so the lesson note is a breakdown of the scheme of work into daily lessons, which are planned by the teacher. In this plan, the teacher explains the step by step procedure which he/she would follow in presenting the lesson to the learners. It usually contains the activities expected of the students as well as the teachers during the period of the lesson. The lesson note (or note of lesson, or lesson plan) is therefore not “curriculum”. The classroom is the implementation point of the programme of learning, which is just one of the three major components of the curriculum. Thus, the lesson note is an important aspect of curriculum implementation, especially the programme of learning component of the curriculum. 3.4 Purpose of School Schools are separated institutions by society for the purpose of transmitting the existing body of knowledge, culture and traditions of the people of a given society to her citizens. Schools are important because they produce boys and girls, men and women that are prepared to live and be integrated into the society. Schools provide manpower, as well as training in character and the virtues which society holds dear. The school is therefore both an agent of stability and change in society. The educated citizenry produced by schools are expected to go back to the society and contribute to its growth and development. However, the school cannot carry out this task without having an adequate and appropriate curriculum designed after due consultation with relevant and appropriate members of the society. Hence the saying that the school and society should both be involved in the curriculum planning process. 5 EDU 703 CURRICULUM DEVELOPMENT: THEORY & PRACTICE 3.5 Nature of Curriculum in School An expert in curriculum called Tyler (1949) was among the first to suggest four fundamental questions which must be answered when talking about the nature of curriculum in schools: These are: 1. What educational purpose should the school seek to attain (Objectives); 2. What educational experiences can be provided to attain these purposes? (i.e. the activities, the subject-matter etc); 3. How can these educational experiences be organized effectively to achieve these purposes? (e.g. Teacher-centered or child-centered learning); 4. How can we determine whether or not the expected objectives have been achieved? (i.e. using tests, performance observations and other forms of evaluation). These four fundamental questions from the core of the curriculum development process. By its very nature therefore curriculum cannot be said have been presented until objectives, contents evaluation procedures are clearly specified. This will be further elaborated in upon subsequent units. 3.6 It Importance of Curriculum in Schools is clear from what has been written so far as you can see, that curriculum is the very heart of the school system. There can be no school if there is no curriculum. Curriculum is the reason for existence of the school. Schools develop their own curriculum, sometimes, from existing planned curriculum, in order to meet its own peculiar needs. For example, in school where truancy is very common, the authorities of the school (which includes the Parents/Teachers Association) may design a package to attract students to school so as to stem the tide of truancy. As the plan used by the school to implement its educational programmes, curriculum is the very vital software without which building and other facilities, (as well as teachers too) will have nothing to do in the school. Self Assessment Questions 1. 2. Explain the term “learning experiences”. What is the purpose of the school? 6 EDU 703 CURRICULUM DEVELOPMENT: THEORY & PRACTICE 3. 4. Make a clear distinction in your own words between curriculum and a. Syllabus b. Scheme of work c. Course of study d. Lesson note Would you agree or disagree that any one of the three programmes that make up the term “curriculum” can stand as “the curriculum” of a school? Give reasons for your answer. 4.0 CONCLUSION The word “curriculum” must be fully understood by educationists so as to avoid confusion which in turn will affect what goes on in schools. The clarifications that have been made in the unit has cleared the confusions. Curriculum must be understood from the totality of programmes or learning experiences organized for learners under the auspices of the schools. Thus, the term curriculum must not be restricted to syllabus, scheme of work or other related terms. These basic concepts must be understood for meaningful implementation of
