text:
Chika’s essay examines the supernatural aspects of his life and his encounter with the spirit forces that interfere with his physical world. It is a very vulnerable and courageous essay which opened up a whole discussion on Igbo cosmology and worldview.
Spirit Wife, Spirit Life
We are legion.
My journey began long before I knew I was on a journey, long before I knew that I was not alone. How old was I? Two? Three? Four?
It is my earliest memory and sometimes it comes across to me through the haze of time as something dreamed—a nightmare shaky with the uncertainty of ancient memory. I was kneeling with my playmates in the sandy compound of our tenement, playing house. My companions were the children of the other tenants of the tenement. I felt a sudden coldness at the back of my neck, as if someone had placed ice on it. I turned and saw a very beautiful fair-complexioned woman in immaculate white lace garment standing near our playground. She was smiling at me. For some reason, I turned to the girl beside me and said, Negodu, Chinenye, look, she’s smiling.
“Who?”
I looked and there was no sign of her anymore. But I was sure she had been there.
***
Odi naabo, which is midnight.
There are other times when I have encountered strange women in my childhood—at school, near a market alley, in a bus. They all looked alike, as if they were born of the same mother. One night, it was a woman’s silhouette near the fridge in our room in the middle of the night; other times, it was a familiar face appearing in my dreams. Unlike my other dreams, there was something inexplicably real about these ones. The background is always somewhat shaky and glassy but the events were clear enough, the landscapes familiar. All the people I saw were kind and beautiful but they evoked fear in me. At some point in my childhood—a point in which my mind must have decided what was real and what was most likely not—I developed a chronic fear of things I could not easily explain: the events of a horror movie, the unusual shape of trees, earth, or animals. I was seeing things and meanings that others did not and, when I tried to explain, no one understood me. I saw complicity in the eyes of dogs and developed a combination of fear and loathing for dogs in my early years.
In the first decade of my life, that fear was a source of humour in my family. It had worried my parents greatly until the day it stopped. My mother tells a very strange tale of that day:
“It was afternoon and you were in the children’s room, sleeping. But your siblings were playing downstairs in the yard. You were always the quiet one. Your Dad and I were in our room talking. You ran into our room shouting, Nkita! Nkita! Nkita! Your Dad was alarmed and asked, ‘Where is it? Chimezie, where is the dog?’ You pointed in the direction of the children’s room. Your Dad started to go towards the room. I wanted to join him but you started crying, so I stayed with you. Your Dad searched the room and the whole flat but there was no sign of any dog. Till today, I cannot explain what happened that day, but that was the day your fear ended. Perhaps your Dad knew more.”
I cannot fully recall the events of that day. But I recall being chased by a ferocious yellow dog—ekeuke—on a lonely bush path. I ran, screaming through a pebbled path. I heard what seemed like laughter or thunder rumbling—or just a series of hollow echoes from the dog’s barking. The dog’s head grew larger with each minute, its bared teeth too (which looked very human); I slowed down as I ran, a difficult strain against the pull of gravity drawing me backward as I forced my legs forward. As the dog was about to pounce on me, I woke up, wild-eyed, jumped down from the bed, and continued running.
***
Otule aghari, which is confusion.
Reconstructing the many strange events of my childhood to better understand my journey to the native Odinani religion of my people is not an easy task. How do you adequately convey the map of a terrain that may seem too fantastic to non-initiates? Yet all religions are conflations of myth, faith, truth, and awareness of the Other. Growing up as a Christian created complexes which I did not understand until much later. There are things about African cultures that a non-African, even today, may never understand. When our writers create works that are labelled “magical realism”, are they creating a whole new world like Tolkien (assuming what he created was new) or did they merely show what had always been there?
Why did my mother drag us through all those many church missions and ministries? Was she looking for a miracle to assuage her suffering as a widow or was she looking for a space in which she could find herself again? After being told several times that her path to success had been blocked by evil people with witchcraft, perhaps what she sought in churches was the elusive thing that she tried to find in the many failed businesses she dabbled into to put food on the table. She genuinely believed that she could pray her hopes into reality. Yet, I suspect that there were moments she despaired with unbelief, wishing for a deeper solution beyond what her battered mental capacity could reach or conjure, for the serene finality of peace. Suffering numbs the poor, it bends them into positions in which they could do nothing but arch their ears, ready to receive any message of succour, however rushed and superficial.
My mother came back one day and said she’d had a terrible encounter.
What happened? I asked.
A woman saw me today outside the market, she said. The woman pointed to my forehead and said I was one of them. She said I had the same children as them and I immediately shouted, Holy Ghost Fire!
I felt the fear in her voice, in the wild glazed redness of her eyes. Who were they? She declared three days prayer and fasting for us, saying that there were evil people and witches after us. Even then, I felt that my mother was running away from a certain knowledge, a revelation so terrible she was too afraid to confront it, hiding behind a performance of Christian fervour. Most of us in Africa seem to be contending daily with the neurosis of our colonial past, so that we tend to demonise what we do not understand about our own selves. What we are really contending with is duality—of our old bloodlines and the modern legacy of Western ways of living and thinking. The curious one, like me, will seek to know the origins of this neurosis, but the vast majority will continue to live with it, staunch in their own belief in the shallow present, morbidly afraid of the dense past, aware that our way is not the easy way.
***
Obala ora, which is a spell.
I have often wondered why many things seem very difficult for me—and not in an existential way. Almost everything I did in my life was a drag, an effort to survive, to scale very low fences. There was the truth of my limbo with my degree certificate at the university, even after years of graduating. There was the reality of not ever having anything my peers had. I have often wondered if there was something against me that I was not aware of—something monumentally metaphysical, real yet unreal, bestowed by unseen beings greater than me, something determined to make me miserable in stages of life others found normal. This thought did not occur to me like an ordinary thought; it occurred to me like déjà vu, as if I knew something about it.
In one of the churches my mother had taken us to in our teenage years, a prophet had once declared that there was a curse on my twin brother and me. At this declaration, my mother nodded with eagerness as if she was on the cusp of a monumental discovery. The prophet would go on to prophesy a deliverance that involved the reading of dozens of psalms, exorcism sessions in church, monetary donations, and incessant prayers. We went through all that and nothing happened. This was the story that repeated itself over the next five years. The only significant thing at that point was that I stopped seeing those strange women. Still, I struggled to anchor my memories to reality. Nothing was ever too sure.
Years later, while living in the village during the pandemic, the loneliness of distancing added new dimensions to my trauma. My dreams came back, acquiring the sinister colour of intangible things. An amorphous figure chased me in my dreams. Sometimes it was a masquerade, sometimes an animal (a cross between dog, leopard, and elephant), and sometimes a nondescript giant. This dream almost always ended the same way: I would run and run and run and eventually come to the edge of a steep cliff, the being still chasing, and then I would jump into the gaping chasm, screaming and falling, arms flailing, without ever hitting the bottom. In another recurring dream I would feel a heavy weight bearing down on my neck, chest, and ribs. I didn’t know what was wrong with me and yet I knew that there was something wrong with me or my surroundings. Sometimes I’d wake up at night and take a walk through the dark deserted streets of the village, past the dark shadow of silent houses, where I would sometimes hear rhythmic snoring from sleeping bodies. I’d continue walking, cold, lonely, and unafraid of the overbearing swish of the tree branches in the night breeze, or the hoot of owls, or the orgies of tiny night voices. In the Igbo traditional belief system, nighttime is a period when spirits merged with the living, when otherworldly beings roamed the earth freely; a persistent nightwalker is sure to stumble upon an insouciant spirit one day. Woe betide him!
One night, I danced to a song by Passenger after getting off a call with my professor who made some life-changing promises to me on the call. After that night, I started seeing cobwebs everywhere I turned. Sometimes, when I went to town, I would walk through a large thoroughfare, where cars and people passed and, inexplicably, I would stumble into a cocoon of cobwebs. Sometimes I would stand near a kerb trying to disentangle my face from the cobwebs that had invaded it and a passerby would ask, What are you doing?
My professor never called again and never picked my calls nor answered my emails. Again and again, I asked myself what my problem was. Was it something in my character, in my aura? Was it beyond me or was it something I could try to get to the bottom of? Surely, no normal person had the experiences I had. I was ill for three weeks afterwards.
When I recovered, I went to Enugu to see my friend Arinze, an amateur footballer. He told me he would take me to see an old woman in his hometown.
 
II
The journey of my self-discovery took me through a long dusty road in the eastern countryside. From Enugu to Amagunze, Arinze’s village, is just 25 kilometres as the crow flies, but there was no functional road through that route. The available road—the Nenwe-Umuowalagu-Akpugo Road—stretched through a meandering route to about 37 kilometres. This was the road we followed. Through the arduous bus journey to Amagunze from the rowdy motor park in Enugu, I was full of the many thoughts and images that troubled me.
At Amagunze, we took a motorbike to a mildly forested area on the other side of the town. Just on the edge of the forest, there was a cluster of mud houses roofed with zinc sheets, as if to declare that the occupants were interested in the old and the new. The matriarch, a thin old woman in her late eighties or early nineties, with her very dark face covered in waves of heavy wrinkles, welcomed us. She took us to her shrine, where the strong smell of incense and a heap of tiny female dolls stared back at us. I could not look at her face, for I felt my heart lurch violently when she lifted a bunch of cowries and threw them on a white plate. It was my first time visiting a dibia.
“You are welcome, nna a, she said, studying the cowries on the plate. You are one of us, do you know?”
I shook my head.
She began to speak in a very low voice, in her dialect. I didn’t understand some of the words and Arinze helped with the occasional translation.
“There are many of us spread all over the world who had entered the world through different channels; there are four channels: water, earth, sun, and wind. There are many things you will not understand yet,” she said, “but the important thing is to know that you are not alone. I see here that there are two of you. Are you a twin?”
Yes, I said.
“That makes it even clearer now.” Her eyes were glued to the cowrie pieces. “Twins are water children, under oath to never be alone, to come together. We have a life elsewhere; we are both here and there. Adjusting to life on this side is always difficult because we are never entirely rooted here. We continue to talk to our companions on the other side. Sometimes they threaten us or haunt us if we are not keeping our own part of the contract. We all signed a spiritual contract, an oath, to be here.”
She lifted the cowries and threw them again on the plate.
“Are you encountering difficulties?”
Difficulties?
“Do you have problems in your life?” she asked.
Arinze explained.
Yes, I said, many problems.
“Having problems is a normal thing, but there are also problems that are not usual.” She was looking straight at me; I tried to avoid her eyes: roundish, strange, dense with glaucoma. “You will have difficulties until you align with your companions and spirit guides. Our life on earth is always difficult if we don’t go through the process of connecting back. It is not easy to connect back…But if you show strength, you will be okay. Our journey here, what we came to do here, is not easy.”
She said that the first thing I needed to do was visit a water body for alignment, preferably the river in my hometown, for that was the portal through which I had entered the world. She listed items for the water alignment—fruits, biscuits, a can of Fanta, red cloth, white cloth, and yellow cloth, fresh eggs, nzu, edo, two fowls (hen and cock). Each of these items would be double, for I had to do this ritual with my twin brother.
I left Amagunze, knowing that I had no money to fulfil these obligations. But I knew something had opened up. A novel knowledge was beckoning, demanding that I take the first step.
***
Ete obara, which is revelation.
I came of age the night I saw myself, my body, on the shores of another realm. I am the same person, the same body, but I am living another life. There is a market on the banks of an emerald river; so many magnificent women, men and children mill around. The men are tall and stately, the women supple-skinned, glassy-eyed, with dark flamboyant hair. There are exchanges going on but I do not see what is being sold.
The dream shifts and I am running around an uncompleted house in the middle of a bush, chasing a fair woman, the same fair woman. We are laughing. Playing. She comes forward, demurs, runs back. Her face changes to vague shapes. But her main face keeps returning. Her fine teeth flashes, her hips bounce as she runs. Her laughter echoes and echoes. Somehow, we are in bed in a windowless room. Her beguiling tenderness draws me in; she’s a knowing temptress. She folds her cool arms around my neck. The feeling is delicious. We move together. I feel heady. Sweetness builds up, like heated gas. We explode.
I woke up sticky and wet.
That was how the dreams started. Over the next few months, I had variations of this dream, each time waking to sticky wetness. I was frustrated. A year before, a girl I was deeply in love with had gone back to her ex. I blamed the nature of my dreams on my chronic celibacy.
Over the next few weeks, I began to chat with a girl I knew from Onitsha on WhatsApp. Two months later, we met and had unsatisfactory sex. Whatever I sought with her lacked emotional connection. She was interested in television and showbiz and left me no room to discuss what I considered deep things. I must have disappointed her too. I was restless and troubled. I wanted to make an effort but there was no way in; we were incompatible.
***
Agwu nshi, which is the spirit of thought, knowledge, and everything in-between.
I visited a dibia afterwards. In his chambers, he cast convoluted ugiri strings and told me that the spirits have said that I was full of otule ose, which in afa divination language meant a combination of bad talk and strong doubts. He said, I was being tormented by my wife from the marine realm whom I had left behind with strong promises while coming to earth. I had not fulfilled any of those promises. I had instead become consumed with doubts about what she was. To appease my spirit wife, I must first follow a process of knowing my chi, the guardian spirit of every Igbo person, and then head to a river.
I went home that day thinking about the recurring images of water, earth, animals, and the fair woman.
***
In Igbo Odinani religion, there seems to be a great emphasis on the individual and the symbols, both personal and collective, surrounding him.
On that basis, I have concluded that there is a deep psychological superstructure in Igbo cosmology and Odinani religion. Myth is excavated not for its lore but to examine its relation to human and non-human phenomena. Ani, the earth, the greatest deity in the Igbo pantheon, is the mother of all creation. All civil laws and statutes of living are based on her. There is Ogwugwu, the mother of justice, with family-based fertility deities such as Omumu beneath her. There is Amadioha, the thunder deity; Anyanwu na Agbara, the dual deity of the sun; Udo, the enforcer of justice; Ekwensu, god of war. There are many other gods and deities, each operating with a set of symbols related to everyday living. Omumu, for instance, has the udara tree as its symbol. The udara fruit is not harvested by hand; it is allowed to fall on its own from the tree. The fruit, when opened, has an uncanny resemblance to the female genitalia. The presence of the tree is associated with fertility and abundance.
The method of afa divination often mixes psychoanalytical methods with psychic ability and spirit possession. The mythic symbols within Igbo cosmology and culture are excavated in relation to the dreams and physical experiences of the person seeking to know something about his life. In his 1903 thesis, On the Psychology and Pathology of So-Called Occult Phenomena, Carl Jung argues that neurasthenia, epilepsy, and hysteria are different states of consciousness comparable to occult phenomena experienced by shamans and spiritual mediums. He observes that science ascribes names—somnambulism, double consciousness, lethargy—to these states to explain their conflicting characteristics.
Are my dreams and trances—if I call them that—different levels of consciousness attained? If the identification of spiritual planes within a metaphysical ontology are just hierarchies of consciousness, then perhaps the entire cosmology of a people is within the realm of the psyche. The only other way to psychologically explain the recurring appearance of the fair woman or my past wet dreams is through Freud’s theory of the sexual neurosis, which stray too far from the Odinani religion, in blaming neurosis on early sexual trauma, which I never experienced.
I discovered that I could make symbolic connections and associations in people’s dreams, and it was as if cryptic inscriptions were opening up to me with new meaning. The discovery of my ability to interpret dreams is one of those things in my life that I cannot explain, but it has helped my soul-searching process. The Igbo believe that dream interpretation is a form of minor afa divination.
***
In “Egwuobi Umuoji”, Theresa Ojialo and her troupe sing about a female relative who was weeping:
And I asked her, what is wrong with you?
And I asked her, what is wrong with you?
She said that her husband left her
and went to the spirit world
She said that her husband left her
and went to the spirit world
And I shouted okoooo
In this world, you stay for a while and join the spirits
And I shouted okoooo
In this world, you stay for a while and join the spirits
I pity the children of this world
I pity the children of this world . . . 
The foundation of our belief system is in what came before us and why we are here. Every person has a purpose. This is what drives the Igbo man rooted in the tradition. We have inverted Descartes’ “I think, therefore I exist” to “Others exist, therefore I exist.” We often speak of the first people, ndi mbu na egede, the ancestors, with great reverence. The moon is all beauty and good auguries. The sun is the arbiter of revelations. But there are malevolent spirits, too, that must be avoided: age mates we left behind on our way here, dead people who did not fulfil their destinies and angrily refuse to go back to the spirit world, malevolent ogbanje whose companions did not keep the oath. When we provoke the deities and our spirits guides, they may punish us with these malevolent spirits (Ogbo nu uke or Akaogheli). We must be in harmony with our chi, our guardian spirit, who will always protect and plead our case in the other world.
We did not appear from a vacuum, empty and unaccompanied. Our essence is full of primordial connections from other realms. We come to the earth through the portal of the akpu-okalete tree, the portal of life, where the last oath is sworn before a final entrance into the world through the four elemental pillars of creation—sun, water, earth, wind (eke, orie, afor, nkwo, respectively). When we pray with the kolanut, we call on those four market days and add the refrain “Iseee” to symbolise that the fifth and final symbol of creation is the human being.
The language of afa divination is deep and has no semblance to spoken Igbo. It is not for the seeker to understand in its raw state. It is for the dibia, who sees what the ordinary man cannot see, to explain in lay language.
***

Akwu ora, which is home.
In July 2022 I visited twin female dibias in Awka with Obumneme, a friend and a dibia in his own right. The twins were identical with large grey dreadlocks falling from their heads all the way to their waists; they wore the same type of loose maroon gowns. They looked strangely familiar, as if I had seen them somewhere before. In the inner chamber, one of them cast her ugiri strings and reminded me to align with my chi and with my water family.
Months later, in Obumneme’s house, while he watched and pointed out things, I lifted a white cockerel to the sun and called upon him to bear me witness on this day: you who cast your eternal light upon the world and nothing hides under you. Then I invoked my chi to come and be with me:
I have finally realised that I am not alone. We are legion. We are everywhere, here and beyond. Come and guide me through this long journey of life. I can deal with the surface but you deal with what is beneath. Guide me down this road littered with evil and difficulties. Repair what there is to repair and make whole again what there is to make whole, for this is the meaning of my name—Chimezie. E

text:
The Advantages and Disadvantages of Remote Work
Remote work is a work arrangement that allows employees to work from the comfort of their homes using their computers while connected to the internet. It also allows companies to work with employees from different locations. Remote work became increasingly popular and adopted by organizations due to the advancement of technology. Although remote work offers many benefits, it also comes with some challenges. Therefore, it is imperative to examine both the advantages and disadvantages of remote work. One of the major benefits of remote work is flexibility. Remote workers have the opportunity to do other things because they can carry their laptops wherever they go. They enjoy the flexibility of working while also attending to personal matters. This results in a better work-life balance, more time with family, and less stress from commuting. Workers are able to save transportation costs and avoid heavy traffic, which makes life more convenient and less stressful. Another benefit remote workers enjoy is increased productivity. Working remotely often comes with fewer office distractions, a quieter environment, and a more comfortable workspace. As a result, workers are able to focus better and work more efficiently from home. For employers, remote work provides the opportunity to hire workers from different locations and access global talent. This gives businesses the chance to increase diversity in the workplace and employ highly skilled individuals regardless of their location. Despite these benefits, remote work also comes with certain challenges. One of the major challenges is communication problems. The nature of remote work does not allow regular face-to-face interaction among workers. Employees mainly communicate through digital communication tools, which can sometimes cause delays in communication, difficulty in teamwork, and misunderstandings during online interactions. For example, in some regions, poor internet connection affects effective communication during virtual meetings. Different time zones can also make communication and collaboration more difficult. Another challenge of remote work is isolation and work-life imbalance. Remote workers often feel lonely because they work alone and miss social interaction with coworkers. They may also find it difficult to separate work from their personal lives, which can result in burnout from overworking. For instance, many remote workers work beyond normal working hours and gradually lose connection with their colleagues. This can negatively affect both their mental health and productivity over time. In conclusion, remote work offers several advantages such as flexibility, increased productivity, reduced transportation expenses, and access to a larger talent pool. However, it also presents challenges including communication problems, overworking, and isolation. To create a healthy balance, proper time management and effective communication are necessary to make remote work successful for both employees and businesses.

text:
AI empowered educators and education in Nigeria: A unique call to leadership to corps members to Drive inclusive AI literacy
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming the world, and education is one of the sectors experiencing the greatest impact. Across the globe, AI tools are helping teachers prepare lessons faster, personalize learning, simplify research, and improve classroom engagement. In Nigeria, where challenges such as inadequate teaching resources, overcrowded classrooms, and unequal access to quality education still exist, AI presents a unique opportunity to bridge educational gaps and promote inclusive learning. However, the successful integration of AI into education requires visionary leadership, especially from young Nigerians serving under the National Youth Service Corps (NYSC). Nigeria’s education system faces numerous challenges, including poor infrastructure, shortage of qualified teachers, and limited access to digital learning tools. Many students in rural communities are still excluded from modern educational opportunities because of poverty and technological limitations. AI-powered education can help address some of these problems by providing access to digital tutors, automated translation tools, personalized learning platforms, and virtual teaching assistants. These technologies can support students with disabilities, improve learning outcomes, and help teachers become more productive. Research has also shown growing interest among Nigerian educators in adopting AI for inclusive classrooms. Educators empowered with AI can perform their duties more effectively and creatively. AI tools such as virtual learning assistants, adaptive learning systems, and content-generation platforms enable teachers to create customized lesson plans that suit the needs of different learners. Students who learn slowly can receive additional support, while advanced learners can access more challenging materials. AI also assists in reducing repetitive tasks like grading and attendance management, thereby allowing teachers to focus more on mentoring and critical thinking activities. Beyond improving classroom teaching, AI can expand educational opportunities to underserved communities. In many rural parts of Nigeria, there is a shortage of experienced teachers, especially in science and technology subjects. Through AI-enabled platforms, students can access quality educational content regardless of their location. Translation and speech-recognition technologies can also help learners who speak different local languages or those living with disabilities. Inclusive AI literacy therefore means ensuring that every Nigerian student, regardless of background, gender, disability, or economic status, has the opportunity to understand and benefit from AI technologies. This is where corps members have a significant role to play. NYSC members are uniquely positioned as agents of change because they serve in schools, local communities, government institutions, and rural areas across the country. Many corps members are graduates with exposure to digital technologies and modern educational practices. They can therefore become leaders in promoting AI literacy and digital inclusion. Recent initiatives by the NYSC and organizations such as the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) have already begun equipping corps members with digital skills and AI-related training. NYSC (National Youth Service Corps) Corps members can drive inclusive AI literacy in several ways. First, they can organize AI awareness workshops in schools and communities to educate students and teachers on the responsible use of AI tools. Secondly, they can introduce learners to free educational platforms that support digital learning and critical thinking. Thirdly, corps members can serve as mentors by teaching basic digital skills such as online research, coding, cybersecurity awareness, and ethical AI usage. Through Community Development Service (CDS) projects, they can also establish computer literacy clubs in schools, particularly in underserved communities. Furthermore, corps members must demonstrate leadership by promoting ethical and responsible AI usage. AI should not become a tool for academic dishonesty or misinformation. Instead, students should be taught how to use AI for creativity, problem-solving, innovation, and research. Educators and learners alike must understand the importance of critical thinking, data privacy, and digital responsibility. AI should complement human intelligence rather than replace it. The Nigerian government, educational institutions, private organizations, and development agencies also have a collective responsibility to support this vision. Investments in electricity, internet access, teacher training, and digital infrastructure are necessary for AI-powered education to succeed. Without proper infrastructure, the dream of inclusive AI literacy may remain inaccessible to many students, especially those in rural communities. Public-private partnerships can help provide affordable devices, digital training, and AI resources for schools nationwide. In conclusion, AI-powered education represents a major opportunity for Nigeria to transform its educational system and prepare young people for the future digital economy. Empowering educators with AI tools can improve teaching quality, enhance learning experiences, and create more inclusive classrooms. However, achieving this vision requires leadership, commitment, and active participation from Nigerian youths, especially corps members. As future leaders and nation-builders, corps members have a unique responsibility to promote inclusive AI literacy and ensure that no student is left behind in the digital age. By embracing innovation, ethical leadership, and community service, they can help build a smarter, more inclusive, and technologically advanced Nigeria.

text:
Pesticides and Fertilizers: Benefits and Environmental Consequences
Fertilisers are agricultural substances that are applied to Nourish plants, enhance soil fertility, and promote growth using nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (NPK). While Pesticides are substance used to Control or kill pests, including insects (insecticides), weeds (herbicides), and fungi (fungicides), protecting crops from damage. Without these nutrients, crops would fail to reach their full potential, resulting in reduced yields and diminished quality. Bill gate said, from his GatesNotes blog post "Why I love fertilizer that Fertilizer is a magical innovation that’s responsible for saving millions of lives from hunger and lifting millions more out of poverty by boosting agricultural productivity. Pesticides also play a crucial role in modern farming by protecting crops from a variety of threats, including insects, weeds, and diseases. By effectively managing these threats, pesticides help farmers maximise their yields, ensuring food security for communities and contributing to the overall economy. Unfortunately, there is an adverse effect on the usage of pesticides and fertilisers in our environment and to the human health. A wealth of studies and reports address the adverse environmental and human health impacts of pesticides and fertilizers, with more information readily available on pesticides. The adverse impacts of pesticides are partly due to the fact that these chemicals are designed to be inherently toxic. While they are designed to eliminate unwanted insects, plants and other living organisms, they may also adversely affect non-target organisms (FAO and World Health Organization [WHO] 2014). In the case of fertilizers, adverse impacts mainly result from their release into the environment and their effects on ecosystems, which are largely due to nutrient loss through overuse or inefficient use (Sutton et al. 2013; FAO 2015; Kopittke et al. 2019). Pesticides affect a diverse range of non-target species and the magnitude of this hazard remains only partially understood. Wan et al. found that insecticides, fungicides and herbicides have negative effects on non-target plants, animals and microorganisms within terrestrial and aquatic systems. Health effects of pesticide may be acute or delayed in those who are exposed. Acute effects can include pesticide poisoning, which may be a medical emergency. Strong evidence exists for other, long-term negative health outcomes from pesticide exposure including birth defects, fetal death, neurodevelopmental disorders, cancer, and neurologic illness including Parkinson's disease. Toxicity of pesticides depend on the type of chemical, route of exposure, dosage, and timing of exposure. Pesticides, herbicides, and fertilizers should be applied sparingly, not exceeding manufacturer’s directions. When handling potential harmful materials such as fertilizers, herbicides, and pesticides, use efficient and safe storage and cleanup methods. Fertilizer management involves control of the rate, timing, and method of application to minimize the chance of polluting surface water or groundwater. Always store in a dry area free from rainfall. In conclusion, pesticides and fertilizers are important in improving agricultural production, but their misuse can seriously damage the environment and human health. Proper management and sustainable farming practices are necessary to reduce their harmful effects while maintaining food security.

text:
The Miseducation of Nigerian Poets
Introduction
Contemporary Nigerian poetry has experienced remarkable visibility in the digital age. Through social media platforms, online journals, literary blogs, and international magazines, Nigerian poets have reached audiences beyond the geographical limitations that constrained earlier generations of writers. This visibility has contributed to the globalization of Nigerian literature and has enabled a new generation of poets to participate actively in international literary conversations. However, alongside these opportunities has emerged a growing concern regarding the direction and identity of contemporary Nigerian poetry. It is within this context that Chukwudera’s argument on “The Miseducation of Nigerian Poets” becomes significant.
Chukwudera contends that although Nigerian poets continue to produce important and aesthetically impressive poetry in the age of social media, many poets have become trapped in what he describes as a “choreography of styles and themes.” According to him, the increasing importance of American literary magazines has encouraged Nigerian poets to imitate foreign poetic patterns, thematic preoccupations, and stylistic conventions. Yet, Chukwudera insists that the real problem is not merely the influence of American magazines themselves; rather, it is the lack of literary consciousness among Nigerian poets that makes them susceptible to external influence. His argument raises important questions concerning literary identity, cultural authenticity, globalization, artistic independence, and the responsibilities of African writers in a rapidly interconnected world.
This essay critically discusses Chukwudera’s position by examining the historical evolution of Nigerian poetry, the influence of globalization and American literary institutions, the role of social media, and the consequences of literary dependency. It also evaluates whether foreign influence necessarily leads to cultural miseducation and argues that the challenge facing Nigerian poets is ultimately one of balance: the ability to engage global literary spaces without abandoning local consciousness and artistic individuality.
Nigerian Poetry and the Question of Identity
Nigerian poetry has historically been rooted in social consciousness and cultural engagement. From the works of pioneers such as Christopher Okigbo, Wole Soyinka, and J. P. Clark to later voices like Niyi Osundare and Tanure Ojaide, Nigerian poetry traditionally reflected indigenous experiences, political struggles, communal realities, and African philosophical worldviews. These poets employed local imagery, oral traditions, folklore, and socio-political commentary to articulate Nigerian realities. Their poetry was not merely an imitation of Western literary traditions but a deliberate effort to create an authentic African literary identity.
The first generation of Nigerian poets wrote during colonial and postcolonial periods marked by political instability, nationalism, military dictatorship, and economic crises. Consequently, poetry became an instrument of resistance and social criticism. Nigerian poets developed voices that were deeply conscious of their historical and cultural responsibilities. Even when influenced by European literary traditions, these writers retained a strong awareness of local realities.
In contrast, contemporary Nigerian poetry exists within a highly globalized literary environment. Young poets now interact daily with international literary communities through social media platforms such as X (formerly Twitter), Instagram, Facebook, and online workshops. American and European literary magazines have become important gateways to recognition, publication, and financial opportunities. While this international exposure has expanded the reach of Nigerian poetry, it has also created pressures for conformity to dominant global literary tastes.
It is this condition that Chukwudera describes as the “miseducation” of Nigerian poets. According to his argument, many poets no longer write primarily from the urgency of local experiences but instead shape their works according to the expectations of foreign literary institutions. Themes such as identity trauma, migration, sexuality, depression, and personal vulnerability—often favored by Western literary magazines—have become increasingly dominant in contemporary Nigerian poetry. As a result, poetry risks becoming performative rather than authentic.
The Influence of American Literary Magazines
The growing influence of American literary magazines on Nigerian poetry cannot be ignored. Publications based in the United States often serve as major platforms for literary validation. Acceptance into prestigious magazines can provide visibility, fellowships, scholarships, and international recognition. Consequently, many Nigerian poets consciously or unconsciously adapt their writing styles to suit editorial preferences within these institutions.
This phenomenon has produced what Chukwudera calls a “choreography of styles and themes.” The phrase suggests uniformity and imitation rather than originality. Many poems begin to resemble one another in tone, imagery, and subject matter because poets internalize what they believe foreign editors want to read. The result is the emergence of formulaic poetry characterized by fragmented structures, confessional narratives, minimalist language, and carefully curated emotional vulnerability.
The issue here is not that these styles are inherently problematic. Literary exchange has always existed across cultures, and no literature develops in complete isolation. African literature itself has evolved partly through engagement with European literary forms. However, the danger arises when imitation replaces innovation and when poets prioritize foreign approval over genuine artistic expression.
Furthermore, the dominance of American literary standards risks marginalizing indigenous poetic traditions. Nigerian oral aesthetics, proverbs, myths, and communal storytelling may gradually disappear from contemporary poetry if poets consider them less marketable internationally. This creates a situation where local experiences are filtered through foreign expectations before being considered worthy of publication.
Chukwudera’s criticism therefore reflects broader postcolonial anxieties concerning cultural dependency. The concern is not simply about influence but about power. American literary institutions possess significant cultural authority, and this authority shapes what kinds of African stories become globally visible. Nigerian poets seeking international success may therefore feel compelled to reproduce narratives that fit Western perceptions of African identity.
Social Media and the Performance of Poetry
The rise of social media has further intensified these dynamics. Social media has democratized literary production by allowing poets to publish instantly and build audiences independently. Young Nigerian poets can now circulate their work globally without relying solely on traditional publishing houses. This development has undoubtedly expanded literary participation and encouraged creative experimentation.
However, social media also encourages performativity and trend-based creativity. Poems are often crafted for instant emotional impact, shareability, and online engagement rather than for depth or lasting artistic value. The pressure to gain visibility may push poets toward fashionable themes and stylistic patterns that attract attention quickly.
In this environment, literary validation becomes tied to virality and international recognition. Poets may begin to measure success through publication in foreign magazines, online popularity, and social media approval rather than through artistic integrity or social relevance. Chukwudera’s argument suggests that this environment contributes to a weakening of literary consciousness. Instead of developing independent artistic identities, poets become consumers and imitators of dominant literary trends.
Nevertheless, social media should not be viewed entirely negatively. It has also enabled important literary communities and discussions among Nigerian writers. Many contemporary poets use digital spaces to address political corruption, gender violence, police brutality, migration, and economic hardship. The #EndSARS movement, for instance, demonstrated how poetry and spoken word performances could serve as instruments of political activism and collective memory in Nigeria.
Therefore, the problem lies not in social media itself but in how poets navigate its pressures and possibilities.
Literary Consciousness and the Responsibility of the Poet
A central aspect of Chukwudera’s argument is the idea of “consciousness.” By this, he refers to the poet’s awareness of history, culture, identity, and artistic responsibility. A conscious poet does not merely imitate dominant trends but critically engages with both local and global influences.
The absence of such consciousness leads to literary dependency. Poets become easily swayed because they lack a firm grounding in their own literary traditions and socio-cultural realities. This concern echoes earlier debates in African literature about cultural alienation and the role of the African writer.
For instance, writers such as Chinua Achebe consistently argued that African literature must remain connected to African experiences and perspectives. Achebe believed that writers should reclaim African narratives from colonial distortions and represent their societies authentically. Similarly, Ngũgĩ wa Thiong’o emphasized the importance of decolonizing African literature and resisting cultural imperialism.
Chukwudera’s argument can therefore be understood as part of a broader intellectual tradition that questions the consequences of excessive dependence on Western literary validation. His criticism does not necessarily reject global engagement but warns against uncritical imitation.
A conscious Nigerian poet should be able to interact with international literary cultures while maintaining originality and cultural rootedness. Such consciousness requires extensive reading, historical awareness, and critical self-reflection. It also demands confidence in the value of Nigerian experiences and indigenous artistic traditions.
Reconsidering the Idea of “Miseducation”
While Chukwudera’s argument is compelling, the concept of “miseducation” may also require critical reconsideration. Literary influence is not inherently negative. Cultures have always evolved through interaction, borrowing, and adaptation. Nigerian poetry itself emerged partly through encounters between African oral traditions and European literary forms. Therefore, exposure to American magazines does not automatically imply cultural loss.
Moreover, contemporary Nigerian poets operate within global realities that differ significantly from those of earlier generations. Migration, digital communication, transnational identities, and multicultural experiences are genuine aspects of modern Nigerian life. Themes of displacement, identity crisis, mental health, and personal vulnerability are therefore not necessarily artificial impositions from the West; they may reflect authentic contemporary experiences.
Additionally, many Nigerian poets successfully combine global influences with local realities. Their works demonstrate that international engagement and cultural authenticity are not mutually exclusive. Rather than rejecting foreign influence entirely, the goal should be to cultivate critical engagement and creative independence.
Thus, the real issue may not be foreign influence itself but the unequal power structures that shape global literary production. American magazines become problematic when they function as gatekeepers determining which African voices gain visibility. The challenge for Nigerian poets is therefore to resist reducing their creativity to market expectations while still participating in global literary conversations.
Conclusion
Chukwudera’s discussion of “The Miseducation of Nigerian Poets” highlights a significant tension within contemporary Nigerian literature. Although Nigerian poets continue to produce important and innovative works in the social media age, the increasing influence of American literary magazines has encouraged conformity to dominant global styles and themes. According to Chukwudera, the deeper problem lies not merely in foreign influence but in the lack of literary consciousness among Nigerian poets, which makes them vulnerable to imitation and dependency.
This essay has shown that Nigerian poetry has historically been grounded in cultural identity, social responsibility, and political consciousness. However, globalization and digital media have transformed literary production, creating new opportunities as well as new pressures. While foreign literary influence is inevitable and not inherently harmful, excessive dependence on external validation can weaken originality and marginalize indigenous artistic traditions.
Ultimately, the future of Nigerian poetry depends on the ability of poets to balance global engagement with local consciousness. Nigerian poets must cultivate intellectual independence, critical awareness, and confidence in their own cultural realities. Only through such consciousness can they participate meaningfully in global literary spaces without losing the uniqueness and authenticity that have historically distinguished Nigerian literature.

text:
RESEARCH PAPER WRITING TIPS
Introduction
Research paper writing is an essential aspect of academic scholarship and intellectual development. It serves as a formal means through which students, scholars, and researchers communicate ideas, investigate problems, and contribute new knowledge to their respective disciplines. A well-written research paper demonstrates critical thinking, analytical ability, originality, and mastery of academic conventions. However, many students encounter difficulties during the research and writing process due to inadequate planning, weak organization, poor citation practices, and insufficient understanding of academic writing standards.
Effective research paper writing goes beyond merely gathering information from different sources. It involves identifying a clear research problem, conducting systematic investigation, organizing evidence logically, and presenting arguments coherently and objectively. Academic writing also requires precision, clarity, formal language, and adherence to ethical standards such as proper referencing and avoidance of plagiarism.
This discussion examines important tips for writing an effective research paper. It explores the major stages involved in academic research writing, including topic selection, literature review, thesis development, organization, citation, editing, and revision. The essay further highlights common mistakes students should avoid and emphasizes the importance of discipline and critical thinking in producing quality academic work.
Understanding the Purpose of a Research Paper
Before beginning a research paper, it is important for the writer to understand its purpose. A research paper is not simply a summary of information gathered from books or websites. Rather, it is an academic document that presents an argument, analysis, interpretation, or investigation based on evidence from credible sources.
Research papers are designed to:
Investigate specific academic or social problems.
Contribute new ideas or perspectives to existing knowledge.
Demonstrate the researcher’s understanding of a topic.
Develop critical and analytical thinking skills.
Communicate findings in a structured and scholarly manner.
Understanding these objectives helps the writer maintain focus and avoid producing descriptive or uncritical work.
Choosing a Suitable Research Topic
One of the most important steps in research paper writing is selecting an appropriate topic. A good research topic determines the direction and quality of the entire paper. Students should choose topics that are clear, manageable, relevant, and researchable.
A suitable topic should possess the following characteristics:
Clarity – The topic should be specific and easy to understand.
Relevance – It should address an important issue within the field of study.
Researchability – There should be enough scholarly materials available on the topic.
Originality – The topic should provide room for fresh analysis or interpretation.
Interest – Writers often perform better when they are genuinely interested in the subject.
For example, a broad topic such as “Education in Nigeria” may be difficult to handle effectively. Narrowing it to “The Impact of Digital Learning on Secondary School Education in Nigeria” makes the study more focused and manageable.
Conducting Preliminary Research
After selecting a topic, the next step is conducting preliminary research. This stage involves gathering background information to understand the topic better and identify existing scholarly discussions. Preliminary research helps the writer refine research questions and determine the scope of the study.
Reliable academic sources include:
Scholarly journals
Academic books
Conference papers
Government publications
Theses and dissertations
Credible online databases
Students should avoid relying heavily on unreliable internet sources, blogs, or unverified websites. Academic credibility is essential in research writing.
Taking organized notes during research is also important. Writers should record important ideas, quotations, page numbers, and bibliographic details to simplify referencing later.
Developing a Strong Thesis Statement
A thesis statement is the central argument or main idea of the research paper. It guides the direction of the discussion and informs readers about the purpose of the study. A weak thesis often leads to poorly organized writing.
An effective thesis statement should be:
Specific
Arguable
Clear
Concise
Focused
For instance, instead of writing:
“Social media affects students,”
a stronger thesis statement would be:
“Excessive use of social media negatively affects the academic performance and concentration levels of university students.”
A strong thesis provides direction and helps maintain coherence throughout the paper.
Preparing an Outline
An outline serves as the structural framework of the research paper. It helps writers organize ideas logically before beginning the actual writing process. Preparing an outline prevents confusion and improves the flow of arguments.
A standard research paper outline usually includes:
Introduction
Literature Review
Methodology
Discussion or Analysis
Conclusion
References
Each section should contain relevant subpoints that support the central thesis.
Writing the Introduction
The introduction is the first section readers encounter; therefore, it should be engaging and informative. A good introduction provides background information, identifies the research problem, states the objectives of the study, and presents the thesis statement.
An effective introduction should:
Introduce the topic clearly.
Explain the significance of the study.
Provide necessary background information.
State the research question or problem.
Present the thesis statement.
The introduction should not be excessively long but should provide enough context for readers to understand the purpose of the paper.
Reviewing Relevant Literature
The literature review examines previous studies related to the research topic. Its purpose is to demonstrate familiarity with existing scholarship and identify gaps the current study intends to address.
A good literature review should:
Summarize relevant scholarly works.
Compare and contrast different viewpoints.
Identify strengths and weaknesses in previous studies.
Show how the current study contributes to existing knowledge.
Students should avoid merely listing authors and summaries without critical analysis. The literature review should be analytical rather than descriptive.
Using Proper Research Methodology
Research methodology refers to the methods used to collect and analyze data. The methodology section is particularly important in scientific and social science research.
Common research methods include:
Surveys
Interviews
Experiments
Observations
Case studies
Content analysis
The chosen methodology should align with the research objectives and questions. Writers should clearly explain:
The research design
Population and sample size
Data collection methods
Data analysis procedures
Clarity in methodology enhances the credibility and reliability of the research.
Maintaining Clarity and Academic Style
Academic writing requires formal language and objective presentation. Writers should avoid slang, contractions, and overly emotional expressions.
Important tips for academic writing include:
Use clear and precise language.
Maintain logical flow between paragraphs.
Avoid unnecessary repetition.
Support claims with evidence.
Use transition words for coherence.
Write in an objective and analytical tone.
Each paragraph should contain one central idea supported by examples, evidence, or scholarly references.
Proper Citation and Referencing
Citation is a crucial aspect of academic writing because it acknowledges the original sources of information and prevents plagiarism. Failure to cite sources properly can result in academic misconduct.
Common citation styles include:
APA (American Psychological Association)
MLA (Modern Language Association)
Chicago Style
Harvard Referencing
Students must follow the citation style specified by their institution or discipline consistently throughout the paper.
Proper referencing involves:
In-text citations
Reference lists or bibliographies
Accurate author and publication details
Writers should also understand the difference between direct quotations, paraphrasing, and summarizing.
Avoiding Plagiarism
Plagiarism occurs when a writer presents another person’s ideas or words as their own without proper acknowledgment. It is considered a serious academic offense.
Ways to avoid plagiarism include:
Citing all borrowed information.
Using quotation marks for direct quotes.
Paraphrasing correctly.
Keeping accurate research notes.
Using plagiarism detection tools when necessary.
Academic honesty is fundamental to credible research writing.
Editing and Proofreading
Many students underestimate the importance of editing and proofreading. First drafts often contain grammatical errors, unclear arguments, and structural weaknesses. Revising the paper improves quality significantly.
During editing, writers should check for:
Clarity of arguments
Organization of ideas
Grammar and spelling errors
Sentence structure
Citation accuracy
Consistency in formatting
Reading the paper aloud can help identify awkward sentences and unclear sections. Peer review and supervisor feedback are also valuable during revision.
Time Management in Research Writing
Research paper writing requires effective time management. Procrastination often leads to rushed work, poor analysis, and avoidable mistakes.
Useful time management strategies include:
Creating a writing schedule
Setting realistic deadlines
Dividing the work into smaller tasks
Starting research early
Allowing enough time for revision
Good planning reduces stress and improves productivity.
Common Mistakes in Research Paper Writing
Several common mistakes weaken research papers. These include:
Choosing topics that are too broad.
Lack of a clear thesis statement.
Poor organization of ideas.
Inadequate evidence or analysis.
Overdependence on quotations.
Weak referencing practices.
Grammatical and spelling errors.
Failure to follow formatting guidelines.
Avoiding these errors enhances the overall quality and credibility of academic work.
Conclusion
Research paper writing is a complex but essential academic skill that requires careful planning, critical thinking, and disciplined execution. A successful research paper is built upon a clear topic, strong thesis statement, credible evidence, proper organization, and accurate referencing. Students must also maintain academic integrity by avoiding plagiarism and adhering to scholarly writing conventions.
Furthermore, effective research writing involves continuous revision, proofreading, and improvement. By mastering these research paper writing tips, students and researchers can produce scholarly works that are coherent, persuasive, informative, and academically credible. Ultimately, strong research writing skills not only contribute to academic success but also enhance intellectual development and professional competence.

text:
Spirit Wife, Spirit Life
Introduction
The relationship between the spiritual and physical worlds has remained a central aspect of African cosmology for centuries. In many African societies, existence is not limited to material reality alone; rather, human life is understood as deeply interconnected with unseen spiritual forces that influence health, relationships, destiny, morality, and communal wellbeing. Within the Igbo worldview particularly, the spiritual realm occupies a powerful position in everyday life, shaping perceptions of existence and explaining experiences that transcend ordinary human understanding. It is within this philosophical and cultural framework that Chika’s essay Spirit Wife, Spirit Life becomes both significant and controversial.
Chika’s essay explores his deeply personal encounters with supernatural forces and the manner in which these forces interfere with his physical and emotional existence. Through a vulnerable and courageous narrative, the essay opens broader discussions on Igbo cosmology, spirituality, destiny, and the tension between modern rationalism and indigenous belief systems. The concept of a “spirit wife” or spiritual spouse—widely discussed in African spiritual traditions—serves as the focal point of the essay’s examination of metaphysical realities and their perceived effects on human relationships and psychological experiences.
The essay’s importance lies not merely in its supernatural claims but in its willingness to engage subjects often dismissed in contemporary intellectual discourse. By narrating experiences commonly regarded as mystical or culturally taboo, Chika challenges readers to reconsider the boundaries between belief, culture, psychology, and spirituality. This discussion therefore critically examines Spirit Wife, Spirit Life by exploring the concepts of Igbo cosmology, spiritual interference, vulnerability in autobiographical writing, the conflict between traditional spirituality and modernity, and the broader cultural implications of discussing supernatural experiences in contemporary African society.
Igbo Cosmology and the Spiritual Universe
To understand the significance of Spirit Wife, Spirit Life, it is necessary to examine the foundations of Igbo cosmology. Traditional Igbo belief systems view existence as a complex interaction between the physical world (uwa) and the spiritual world inhabited by ancestors, deities, spirits, and metaphysical forces. Unlike Western materialist perspectives that sharply separate the natural from the supernatural, Igbo cosmology perceives both realms as interconnected and mutually influential.
In this worldview, human beings are not isolated entities but participants in a broader cosmic order. Spiritual forces are believed to influence fertility, success, illness, relationships, and destiny. Concepts such as chi (personal spirit or destiny), ancestors, reincarnation, and spiritual covenants form important aspects of Igbo metaphysical understanding.
The idea of spirit spouses emerges from this cosmological framework. A spirit wife or spirit husband is believed to be a supernatural entity spiritually attached to an individual, often influencing romantic relationships, marriage, sexuality, emotional wellbeing, or personal fortune. In many African spiritual traditions, spirit spouses are associated with recurring relationship difficulties, dreams, emotional disturbances, or patterns of misfortune.
Chika’s essay situates itself within this indigenous worldview by presenting supernatural experiences not merely as symbolic metaphors but as realities perceived to affect daily life. His narrative reflects the enduring relevance of traditional cosmological beliefs even within contemporary African societies shaped by Christianity, urbanization, and Western education.
Supernatural Interference and Human Experience
One of the central themes of Spirit Wife, Spirit Life is the idea of supernatural interference in human affairs. Chika describes encounters with invisible spiritual forces that intrude upon his physical existence and emotional life. Such experiences challenge dominant rationalist assumptions that reality can only be understood through empirical evidence and scientific explanation.
Within African spirituality, supernatural interference is often interpreted as evidence of spiritual imbalance, ancestral involvement, or metaphysical attachment. Dreams, unexplained emotional experiences, recurring patterns of failure, and unusual psychological states may be viewed not simply as psychological phenomena but as manifestations of spiritual realities.
The essay becomes particularly compelling because of its deeply personal nature. Rather than presenting abstract theological arguments, Chika narrates lived experiences marked by fear, confusion, vulnerability, and introspection. This autobiographical approach humanizes subjects frequently treated with skepticism or ridicule.
At the same time, the essay raises important intellectual questions concerning the interpretation of supernatural experiences. Modern psychology may interpret certain spiritual encounters as manifestations of trauma, anxiety, subconscious fears, or cultural conditioning. Religious traditions may explain them differently depending on doctrinal perspectives. Consequently, Spirit Wife, Spirit Life occupies a complex space between spirituality, psychology, religion, and cultural identity.
The essay’s strength lies in its refusal to simplify these experiences. Instead, it invites readers to confront the ambiguity of human existence and acknowledge that many societies continue to interpret reality through metaphysical frameworks that differ from Western secular thought.
Vulnerability and Courage in Personal Writing
Another remarkable aspect of Chika’s essay is its emotional vulnerability. Writing publicly about supernatural experiences, particularly within modern intellectual environments, requires considerable courage. Discussions about spirit spouses are often surrounded by stigma, skepticism, or accusations of irrationality. Consequently, individuals who openly narrate such experiences expose themselves to criticism and misunderstanding.
Chika’s willingness to share deeply personal spiritual struggles transforms the essay into more than a discussion of metaphysical beliefs; it becomes an exploration of fear, identity, loneliness, and emotional conflict. Vulnerability in autobiographical writing creates emotional authenticity and encourages readers to engage empathetically with experiences outside their own worldview.
Furthermore, the essay contributes to broader conversations about mental and spiritual wellbeing in African societies. Many individuals experiencing unexplained emotional or psychological distress often interpret such experiences through spiritual frameworks inherited from cultural traditions. By openly discussing these realities, Chika creates space for dialogue on subjects frequently silenced or dismissed.
The essay also demonstrates the power of personal narrative in African literature. African autobiographical writing has historically served as a medium for exploring identity, trauma, spirituality, colonialism, and social transformation. In this context, Spirit Wife, Spirit Life extends the tradition of using personal experience to illuminate broader cultural and philosophical questions.
Traditional Spirituality and Modern Rationalism
A significant tension explored implicitly in the essay is the conflict between traditional spirituality and modern rationalism. Contemporary African societies exist at the intersection of multiple belief systems, including indigenous religions, Christianity, Islam, and Western scientific thought. As a result, many Africans navigate complex spiritual identities shaped by both traditional cosmology and modern education.
Western modernity often dismisses supernatural claims as superstition or irrationality. Scientific paradigms prioritize empirical evidence and material explanations, leaving little room for metaphysical interpretations of reality. Consequently, beliefs concerning spirit spouses or spiritual interference are frequently marginalized within academic and intellectual discourse.
However, Chika’s essay challenges this dismissal by insisting on the validity of lived spiritual experiences. Whether readers interpret these experiences literally, psychologically, or symbolically, the essay demonstrates that spiritual belief remains deeply influential in shaping human consciousness and social behavior.
The tension between rationalism and spirituality also reflects broader postcolonial questions concerning knowledge production. Colonialism often portrayed African spiritual systems as primitive or irrational while privileging Western epistemologies as universally superior. Contemporary African writers increasingly challenge this hierarchy by reclaiming indigenous knowledge systems and asserting the legitimacy of African cosmological perspectives.
Thus, Spirit Wife, Spirit Life participates in a larger intellectual movement aimed at decolonizing knowledge and recognizing the diversity of human ways of understanding existence.
Igbo Worldview and Cultural Identity
The essay further contributes to discussions about Igbo identity and cultural continuity. Despite modernization and globalization, traditional beliefs continue to shape social attitudes and personal identities within many African communities. Igbo cosmology remains influential not only in rural settings but also among educated urban populations.
Chika’s narrative demonstrates how cultural beliefs survive and adapt within changing social environments. The persistence of concepts such as spirit spouses reflects the enduring relevance of indigenous spirituality in interpreting human experiences that appear mysterious or emotionally overwhelming.
Additionally, the essay highlights the communal nature of African cosmology. In many African traditions, personal experiences are rarely understood in purely individualistic terms. Spiritual disturbances may involve family histories, ancestral relationships, communal obligations, or inherited destinies. This contrasts with Western individualism, which often emphasizes personal autonomy and psychological explanation.
By engaging these themes, Spirit Wife, Spirit Life becomes more than a personal confession; it serves as a cultural document reflecting the complexities of contemporary African spirituality and identity.
Critical Reflections on the Essay
While the essay is intellectually and emotionally powerful, it also invites critical reflection. One challenge in discussing supernatural experiences is the difficulty of distinguishing between metaphysical interpretation and psychological explanation. Readers approaching the essay from secular or scientific perspectives may question the objective reality of spirit spouses and interpret the experiences differently.
Nevertheless, the significance of the essay does not depend entirely on whether its supernatural claims can be empirically verified. Literature often derives meaning from symbolic, emotional, and cultural truth rather than scientific proof. The essay’s value lies in its exploration of belief, fear, identity, and the human search for meaning within uncertain realities.
Moreover, discussions about spirituality should be approached with sensitivity and intellectual openness. Dismissing indigenous beliefs outright risks reproducing colonial attitudes that delegitimize African knowledge systems. At the same time, unquestioning acceptance of all supernatural claims may discourage critical inquiry. A balanced interpretation therefore recognizes both the cultural significance and interpretive complexity of such experiences.
Conclusion
Spirit Wife, Spirit Life is a courageous and thought-provoking essay that examines the intersection between spirituality, culture, and personal experience within the framework of Igbo cosmology. Through vulnerable autobiographical narration, Chika explores supernatural encounters and the perceived influence of spiritual forces on human existence. In doing so, the essay opens important discussions concerning African spirituality, indigenous knowledge systems, and the relationship between the spiritual and physical worlds.
The essay demonstrates that traditional cosmological beliefs continue to shape contemporary African identities despite the influence of modernity and Western rationalism. It also reveals the emotional and psychological complexities involved in navigating spiritual experiences within societies where such subjects are often stigmatized or misunderstood.
Ultimately, Spirit Wife, Spirit Life challenges readers to reconsider simplistic distinctions between reality and spirituality, modernity and tradition, rationalism and belief. Whether interpreted literally, symbolically, or psychologically, the essay remains an important contribution to discussions about African worldview, cultural identity, and the enduring human desire to understand the mysteries of existence.
